Disk survey (threshold 85%): 228 GB: 61%
Heat: rbk-21-win-theurge-tabarget-auditing (₣BC)
Status: stabled
Created: 260428


## Paces

### calibrant-bash-blackbox-driver (₢BCAAA) [rough]

Drafted from ₢BBAAi in ₣BB.

## Character

BUK-side testbench wiring that black-box-drives the calibrant fixtures (Pace BBAAh) through the `rbtd` binary. Asserts CLI exit codes, stderr diagnostic format, fail-fast policy across fixture and suite layers, and the disposition × keep-going gate landed in BBAAd. The bash layer covers what Rust unit tests cannot honestly self-test: the binary's operator-facing surface and the bash bridge's reaction to verdicts.

Intent-only docket. Section/case catalog locked in paddock — see "Bash blackbox calibrant — design." Case organization, assertion helpers, exact zipper colophon, and tabtarget naming chosen at mount time.

## Scope

- New module `Tools/rbk/rbtt_testbench.sh` registering 14 cases across 6 sections per paddock catalog.
- Six case files in `Tools/rbk/rbts/rbtc*_*.sh` — one per section.
- Zipper entry in `rbz_zipper.sh` mapping new colophon → testbench module.
- One tabtarget stub in `tt/` (one-line exec, with-the-grain).
- Release runbook entry documenting `buw-st` → new-colophon sequence.

## Out of scope

- Rust-side fixture work (Pace BBAAh).
- Cross-kit composition (BUK fixtures registered in rbk testbench).
- Orchestrating tabtargets that exec multiple children.
- Explicit BURV-chain regression tests (incidentally exercised; no dedicated cases).

## Depends on

Pace BBAAh (calibrant-fixture-foundation) — needs the calibrant fixtures registered in the manifest and the `calibrant` suite available before bash assertions can pass.

### theurge-manifest-coverage-check (₢BCAAB) [rough]

Drafted from ₢BBAAj in ₣BB.

## Character

Tighten rbtdrm's per-fixture required-colophons list from "asserted superset" to "exhaustive subset". Two runtime checks, both at the rbtdri_invoke_* primitive chokepoint:

- Positive: every invoked colophon must be in the fixture's declared list — refuse otherwise.
- Negative: every declared colophon must have been invoked at least once on a successful full-fixture run — fail otherwise.

Surgical refactor; no case-body edits expected. The audit risk for the negative check surfaces at turn-on; existing TestSuite runs serve as the audit gate.

## Scope

- rbtdri_Context tracks declared and used colophon sets
- All three invoke primitives (rbtdri_invoke, _global, _imprint) refuse undeclared invocations and record successful ones
- run_suite enforces the negative check after run_sections returns success (gated on result.failed == 0 — failure paths suppress the check)
- run_single enforces positive only — single-case cannot satisfy exhaustiveness by construction
- Unit tests covering both check directions and the single-case exemption
- Operator-facing diagnostics name the fixture and the offending colophon

## Cross-pace dependencies

None. The check applies to all theurge fixtures, independent of gauntlet assembly.

## Out of scope

- Imprint coverage (which nameplate a per-imprint colophon was called with), argument patterns, compile-time/static analysis
- Phase-split into two paces — implement both checks together; the TestSuite run is the validation gate
- Any fixture-level declaration edits — the implementation should pass clean against current declarations or surface a real stale entry as a separate find

### calibrant-coverage-foundation (₢BCAAC) [rough]

## Character

Rust extension to the calibrant family proving BCAAB's runtime manifest-coverage checks land their diagnostics. Three synthetic fixtures with deliberately aligned or mis-declared manifests; one synthetic theurge-internal noop tabtarget the fixtures invoke without cloud or filesystem side-effects. Self-contained — bash driver pace consumes this but does not land here.

Intent-only docket. Catalog locked below; module placement, tabtarget naming, manifest constant naming chosen at mount time.

## Scope

- Synthetic noop tabtarget, theurge-internal (rbtd-l* family — parity with rbtdrl_calibrant.rs).
- Three new fixtures in the calibrant family (Independent disposition):
  - calibrant-coverage-aligned: manifest declares noop, case invokes noop → Pass.
  - calibrant-coverage-undeclared: manifest declares nothing, case invokes noop → fixture-level FAIL via positive check.
  - calibrant-coverage-unused: manifest declares noop, case invokes nothing → fixture-level FAIL via negative check.
- Manifest entries + disposition tags.
- Rust unit tests pin only registration shape; verdict assertions live in the bash driver pace.

## Depends on

- ₣BB BBAAh (calibrant-fixture-foundation) — needs rbtdrl_calibrant.rs and the calibrant-family scaffolding.
- BCAAB (theurge-manifest-coverage-check) — without the runtime checks landed, the failing fixtures pass instead of fail.

## Out of scope

- Bash assertions on exit code or stderr shape — sibling pace.
- Inclusion in the calibrant suite — these fixtures are standalone-invoked so the existing suite-fail-fast assertions remain clean.
- Cross-kit composition.

### calibrant-coverage-driver (₢BCAAD) [rough]

## Character

BUK-side testbench extension that black-box-drives the three coverage fixtures from the sibling Rust pace (BCAAC) through the rbtd binary. Asserts CLI exit codes and stderr diagnostic shapes for the positive check (invoked-not-declared) and the negative check (declared-not-invoked), plus the single-case exemption for the negative check.

Intent-only docket. Section and case catalog below; case-file naming and assertion helpers chosen at mount time.

## Scope

- New section manifest-coverage in rbtt_testbench.sh, four cases:
  - coverage-aligned-passes — Run.calibrant-coverage-aligned exits 0.
  - coverage-undeclared-fails — Run.calibrant-coverage-undeclared exits non-zero; stderr matches the positive-check diagnostic naming the offending colophon.
  - coverage-unused-fails — Run.calibrant-coverage-unused exits non-zero; stderr names the unused colophon.
  - coverage-single-case-skips-negative — SingleCase invocation of calibrant-coverage-unused's case exits 0 (proves the single-case exemption).
- Case files in Tools/rbk/rbts/ following BCAAA convention.
- Tabtarget(s) where manifest-driven generation yields them; testbench colophon registration.

## Depends on

- BCAAC (calibrant-coverage-foundation) — needs the three fixtures and the noop tabtarget.
- BCAAA (calibrant-bash-blackbox-driver) — needs rbtt_testbench.sh and the case-file convention.
- BCAAB (theurge-manifest-coverage-check) — transitively, via the Rust fixtures.

## Out of scope

- Rust-side fixture work (sibling pace).
- New testbench infrastructure.
- Trace-file shape contracts beyond exit code + stderr substring.

File-touch bitmap: (no work file changes)

content.content:
1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BC
2	
3	## Context
4	
5	Split from ₣BB rbk-mvp-3-release-qualification to decouple theurge framework self-test infrastructure from the gauntlet release ladder. ₣BB stays focused on canonical-establish → onboarding-sequence → suite-assembly → first-run; this heat owns framework-polish work that doesn't qualify mvp-3 but tightens the framework that runs it. Both heats can race independently once the calibrant fixture foundation lands in ₣BB.
6	
7	## Cross-heat coupling
8	
9	Mounts after ₣BB's BBAAh (calibrant fixture foundation) commits. The four fixtures BBAAh registers — `calibrant-verdicts`, `calibrant-fail-fast`, `calibrant-progressing`, `calibrant-sentinel` — are this heat's blackbox subjects. ₣BB's paddock § "Calibrant fixture family — BBAAh" and the BBAAh commit carry the fixture catalog, anchor-word rationale, and module-rename decisions; mount-time agents read those for fixture-contract detail rather than restating here.
10	
11	## Origin gaps — two design pillars
12	
13	**Operator-surface validation gap (BCAAA).** Rust unit tests verify engine functions in isolation; nothing verifies the operator-facing surface of the `rbtd` binary — CLI exit codes, stderr diagnostic format, fixture and suite fail-fast, the disposition × keep-going policy gate landed in BBAAd. The calibrant family provides synthetic fixtures with deterministic verdicts; this heat drives them through the binary as a black box.
14	
15	**Manifest-coverage drift gap (BCAAB → BCAAC → BCAAD).** rbtdrm's per-fixture `required_colophons` list is asserted-superset — declarations may carry stale entries (declared but never invoked) or missing entries (invoked but not declared) without surfacing. Two runtime checks at the `rbtdri_invoke_*` chokepoint tighten the contract: positive (invoked must be declared) refuses unknown invocations; negative (declared must be invoked) fails on dead declarations after a successful full-fixture run. Coverage-validation fixtures (BCAAC) and a bash driver (BCAAD) pin diagnostic shape.
16	
17	## Bash case catalog — operator-surface validation (BCAAA)
18	
19	`Tools/rbk/rbtt_testbench.sh`, 14 cases across 6 sections:
20	
21	- `verdict-propagation` (4) — pass / fail / skip exit codes + trace file
22	- `fixture-fail-fast` (2) — intra-section, inter-section
23	- `disposition-policy` (3) — Independent + keep-going runs all; StateProgressing + keep-going refused with policy stderr; StateProgressing default runs fail-fast
24	- `probe-diagnostics` (1) — unmet-probe stderr contains `precondition '%s' not met:` + `remediation:`
25	- `suite-fail-fast` (2) — suite aborts on failing fixture; subsequent fixture's sentinel absent
26	- `cli-surface` (2) — unknown fixture errors clearly; missing arg → usage
27	
28	## Coverage-validation case catalog (BCAAC + BCAAD)
29	
30	Three coverage-fixtures (Independent disposition) registered alongside the BBAAh family, exercising both check directions of the manifest-coverage gate:
31	
32	- `calibrant-coverage-aligned` — manifest declares noop, case invokes noop → Pass
33	- `calibrant-coverage-undeclared` — manifest declares nothing, case invokes noop → fixture-level FAIL via positive check
34	- `calibrant-coverage-unused` — manifest declares noop, case invokes nothing → fixture-level FAIL via negative check
35	
36	Bash driver (BCAAD) — section `manifest-coverage`, 4 cases: `coverage-aligned-passes`, `coverage-undeclared-fails`, `coverage-unused-fails`, `coverage-single-case-skips-negative` (proves the `run_single` exemption from the negative check).
37	
38	## Locked design decisions
39	
40	- **Anchor word `calibrant`**, module `rbtdrl_calibrant.rs` (classifier `l` per RCG terminal exclusivity — `c` is owned by crucible). Set in BBAAh; this heat consumes it.
41	- **With-the-grain composition** — tabtargets are bare exec stubs; orchestration lives in zippers / workbenches / testbenches / suites. BUK's self-test stays untouched. No "BUK + calibrant" composite tabtarget — operator sequences `buw-st` then the new colophon.
42	- **No per-fixture tabtargets for calibrant family** — framework-test plumbing, not operator workflows. The bash testbench invokes `rbtd` directly; deviates from BBAAe's per-fixture pattern, justified because pristine / canonical are operator-facing and calibrant isn't.
43	- **BURV chain is contract, not hook** — `BURV_TEMP_ROOT_DIR` / `BURV_OUTPUT_ROOT_DIR` (BUS0 §540-552) is load-bearing infrastructure. Calibrant cases nest BURV through `rbtd` invocations and incidentally regression-test the chain.
44	- **Manifest-coverage check enforcement (BCAAB):**
45	  - Positive at all three `rbtdri_invoke_*` primitives — refuses undeclared invocations
46	  - Negative in `run_suite` after `run_sections` returns success — gated on `result.failed == 0` (failure paths suppress)
47	  - `run_single` enforces positive only — cannot satisfy exhaustiveness by construction
48	
49	## Out of scope
50	
51	- Cross-kit testbench composition (BUK fixtures registered in rbk testbench)
52	- Orchestrating tabtargets containing exec-of-children logic
53	- Trace-file format invariants beyond a single `pass_with_output` smoke check
54	- Color rendering / terminal width contracts
55	- Imprint coverage (which nameplate a per-imprint colophon was called with), argument patterns, compile-time / static analysis
56	- Fixture-level declaration edits in existing fixtures (canonical-establish, etc.) — surface as separate finds if BCAAB's negative check turns up stale entries during ₣BB's gauntlet run
57	
58	## References
59	
60	- ₣BB paddock § "Calibrant fixture family — BBAAh" — anchor-word rationale, module-rename decision, Rust fixture catalog
61	- ₣BB BBAAh — calibrant fixture foundation (prerequisite)
62	- BBAAd — engine concepts (`rbtdre_Disposition`, `rbtdre_resolve_fail_fast`, `rbtdrb_Probe`)
63	- `rbtdri_invoke`, `rbtdri_invoke_global`, `rbtdri_invoke_imprint` — chokepoint primitives BCAAB modifies
64	- `Tools/rbk/rbts/` — case-file convention (BCAAA establishes; BCAAD extends)
65	- BUS0 §540-552 — BURV chain contract
66	
67	# jjezs_pace ₢BCAAA
68	
69	Drafted from ₢BBAAi in ₣BB.
70	
71	## Character
72	
73	BUK-side testbench wiring that black-box-drives the calibrant fixtures (Pace BBAAh) through the `rbtd` binary. Asserts CLI exit codes, stderr diagnostic format, fail-fast policy across fixture and suite layers, and the disposition × keep-going gate landed in BBAAd. The bash layer covers what Rust unit tests cannot honestly self-test: the binary's operator-facing surface and the bash bridge's reaction to verdicts.
74	
75	Intent-only docket. Section/case catalog locked in paddock — see "Bash blackbox calibrant — design." Case organization, assertion helpers, exact zipper colophon, and tabtarget naming chosen at mount time.
76	
77	## Scope
78	
79	- New module `Tools/rbk/rbtt_testbench.sh` registering 14 cases across 6 sections per paddock catalog.
80	- Six case files in `Tools/rbk/rbts/rbtc*_*.sh` — one per section.
81	- Zipper entry in `rbz_zipper.sh` mapping new colophon → testbench module.
82	- One tabtarget stub in `tt/` (one-line exec, with-the-grain).
83	- Release runbook entry documenting `buw-st` → new-colophon sequence.
84	
85	## Out of scope
86	
87	- Rust-side fixture work (Pace BBAAh).
88	- Cross-kit composition (BUK fixtures registered in rbk testbench).
89	- Orchestrating tabtargets that exec multiple children.
90	- Explicit BURV-chain regression tests (incidentally exercised; no dedicated cases).
91	
92	## Depends on
93	
94	Pace BBAAh (calibrant-fixture-foundation) — needs the calibrant fixtures registered in the manifest and the `calibrant` suite available before bash assertions can pass.
95	
96	# jjezs_pace ₢BCAAB
97	
98	Drafted from ₢BBAAj in ₣BB.
99	
100	## Character
101	
102	Tighten rbtdrm's per-fixture required-colophons list from "asserted superset" to "exhaustive subset". Two runtime checks, both at the rbtdri_invoke_* primitive chokepoint:
103	
104	- Positive: every invoked colophon must be in the fixture's declared list — refuse otherwise.
105	- Negative: every declared colophon must have been invoked at least once on a successful full-fixture run — fail otherwise.
106	
107	Surgical refactor; no case-body edits expected. The audit risk for the negative check surfaces at turn-on; existing TestSuite runs serve as the audit gate.
108	
109	## Scope
110	
111	- rbtdri_Context tracks declared and used colophon sets
112	- All three invoke primitives (rbtdri_invoke, _global, _imprint) refuse undeclared invocations and record successful ones
113	- run_suite enforces the negative check after run_sections returns success (gated on result.failed == 0 — failure paths suppress the check)
114	- run_single enforces positive only — single-case cannot satisfy exhaustiveness by construction
115	- Unit tests covering both check directions and the single-case exemption
116	- Operator-facing diagnostics name the fixture and the offending colophon
117	
118	## Cross-pace dependencies
119	
120	None. The check applies to all theurge fixtures, independent of gauntlet assembly.
121	
122	## Out of scope
123	
124	- Imprint coverage (which nameplate a per-imprint colophon was called with), argument patterns, compile-time/static analysis
125	- Phase-split into two paces — implement both checks together; the TestSuite run is the validation gate
126	- Any fixture-level declaration edits — the implementation should pass clean against current declarations or surface a real stale entry as a separate find
127	
128	# jjezs_pace ₢BCAAC
129	
130	## Character
131	
132	Rust extension to the calibrant family proving BCAAB's runtime manifest-coverage checks land their diagnostics. Three synthetic fixtures with deliberately aligned or mis-declared manifests; one synthetic theurge-internal noop tabtarget the fixtures invoke without cloud or filesystem side-effects. Self-contained — bash driver pace consumes this but does not land here.
133	
134	Intent-only docket. Catalog locked below; module placement, tabtarget naming, manifest constant naming chosen at mount time.
135	
136	## Scope
137	
138	- Synthetic noop tabtarget, theurge-internal (rbtd-l* family — parity with rbtdrl_calibrant.rs).
139	- Three new fixtures in the calibrant family (Independent disposition):
140	  - calibrant-coverage-aligned: manifest declares noop, case invokes noop → Pass.
141	  - calibrant-coverage-undeclared: manifest declares nothing, case invokes noop → fixture-level FAIL via positive check.
142	  - calibrant-coverage-unused: manifest declares noop, case invokes nothing → fixture-level FAIL via negative check.
143	- Manifest entries + disposition tags.
144	- Rust unit tests pin only registration shape; verdict assertions live in the bash driver pace.
145	
146	## Depends on
147	
148	- ₣BB BBAAh (calibrant-fixture-foundation) — needs rbtdrl_calibrant.rs and the calibrant-family scaffolding.
149	- BCAAB (theurge-manifest-coverage-check) — without the runtime checks landed, the failing fixtures pass instead of fail.
150	
151	## Out of scope
152	
153	- Bash assertions on exit code or stderr shape — sibling pace.
154	- Inclusion in the calibrant suite — these fixtures are standalone-invoked so the existing suite-fail-fast assertions remain clean.
155	- Cross-kit composition.
156	
157	# jjezs_pace ₢BCAAD
158	
159	## Character
160	
161	BUK-side testbench extension that black-box-drives the three coverage fixtures from the sibling Rust pace (BCAAC) through the rbtd binary. Asserts CLI exit codes and stderr diagnostic shapes for the positive check (invoked-not-declared) and the negative check (declared-not-invoked), plus the single-case exemption for the negative check.
162	
163	Intent-only docket. Section and case catalog below; case-file naming and assertion helpers chosen at mount time.
164	
165	## Scope
166	
167	- New section manifest-coverage in rbtt_testbench.sh, four cases:
168	  - coverage-aligned-passes — Run.calibrant-coverage-aligned exits 0.
169	  - coverage-undeclared-fails — Run.calibrant-coverage-undeclared exits non-zero; stderr matches the positive-check diagnostic naming the offending colophon.
170	  - coverage-unused-fails — Run.calibrant-coverage-unused exits non-zero; stderr names the unused colophon.
171	  - coverage-single-case-skips-negative — SingleCase invocation of calibrant-coverage-unused's case exits 0 (proves the single-case exemption).
172	- Case files in Tools/rbk/rbts/ following BCAAA convention.
173	- Tabtarget(s) where manifest-driven generation yields them; testbench colophon registration.
174	
175	## Depends on
176	
177	- BCAAC (calibrant-coverage-foundation) — needs the three fixtures and the noop tabtarget.
178	- BCAAA (calibrant-bash-blackbox-driver) — needs rbtt_testbench.sh and the case-file convention.
179	- BCAAB (theurge-manifest-coverage-check) — transitively, via the Rust fixtures.
180	
181	## Out of scope
182	
183	- Rust-side fixture work (sibling pace).
184	- New testbench infrastructure.
185	- Trace-file shape contracts beyond exit code + stderr substring.

# jjezs_paddock ₣BC

## Context

Split from ₣BB rbk-mvp-3-release-qualification to decouple theurge framework self-test infrastructure from the gauntlet release ladder. ₣BB stays focused on canonical-establish → onboarding-sequence → suite-assembly → first-run; this heat owns framework-polish work that doesn't qualify mvp-3 but tightens the framework that runs it. Both heats can race independently once the calibrant fixture foundation lands in ₣BB.

## Cross-heat coupling

Mounts after ₣BB's BBAAh (calibrant fixture foundation) commits. The four fixtures BBAAh registers — `calibrant-verdicts`, `calibrant-fail-fast`, `calibrant-progressing`, `calibrant-sentinel` — are this heat's blackbox subjects. ₣BB's paddock § "Calibrant fixture family — BBAAh" and the BBAAh commit carry the fixture catalog, anchor-word rationale, and module-rename decisions; mount-time agents read those for fixture-contract detail rather than restating here.

## Origin gaps — two design pillars

**Operator-surface validation gap (BCAAA).** Rust unit tests verify engine functions in isolation; nothing verifies the operator-facing surface of the `rbtd` binary — CLI exit codes, stderr diagnostic format, fixture and suite fail-fast, the disposition × keep-going policy gate landed in BBAAd. The calibrant family provides synthetic fixtures with deterministic verdicts; this heat drives them through the binary as a black box.

**Manifest-coverage drift gap (BCAAB → BCAAC → BCAAD).** rbtdrm's per-fixture `required_colophons` list is asserted-superset — declarations may carry stale entries (declared but never invoked) or missing entries (invoked but not declared) without surfacing. Two runtime checks at the `rbtdri_invoke_*` chokepoint tighten the contract: positive (invoked must be declared) refuses unknown invocations; negative (declared must be invoked) fails on dead declarations after a successful full-fixture run. Coverage-validation fixtures (BCAAC) and a bash driver (BCAAD) pin diagnostic shape.

## Bash case catalog — operator-surface validation (BCAAA)

`Tools/rbk/rbtt_testbench.sh`, 14 cases across 6 sections:

- `verdict-propagation` (4) — pass / fail / skip exit codes + trace file
- `fixture-fail-fast` (2) — intra-section, inter-section
- `disposition-policy` (3) — Independent + keep-going runs all; StateProgressing + keep-going refused with policy stderr; StateProgressing default runs fail-fast
- `probe-diagnostics` (1) — unmet-probe stderr contains `precondition '%s' not met:` + `remediation:`
- `suite-fail-fast` (2) — suite aborts on failing fixture; subsequent fixture's sentinel absent
- `cli-surface` (2) — unknown fixture errors clearly; missing arg → usage

## Coverage-validation case catalog (BCAAC + BCAAD)

Three coverage-fixtures (Independent disposition) registered alongside the BBAAh family, exercising both check directions of the manifest-coverage gate:

- `calibrant-coverage-aligned` — manifest declares noop, case invokes noop → Pass
- `calibrant-coverage-undeclared` — manifest declares nothing, case invokes noop → fixture-level FAIL via positive check
- `calibrant-coverage-unused` — manifest declares noop, case invokes nothing → fixture-level FAIL via negative check

Bash driver (BCAAD) — section `manifest-coverage`, 4 cases: `coverage-aligned-passes`, `coverage-undeclared-fails`, `coverage-unused-fails`, `coverage-single-case-skips-negative` (proves the `run_single` exemption from the negative check).

## Locked design decisions

- **Anchor word `calibrant`**, module `rbtdrl_calibrant.rs` (classifier `l` per RCG terminal exclusivity — `c` is owned by crucible). Set in BBAAh; this heat consumes it.
- **With-the-grain composition** — tabtargets are bare exec stubs; orchestration lives in zippers / workbenches / testbenches / suites. BUK's self-test stays untouched. No "BUK + calibrant" composite tabtarget — operator sequences `buw-st` then the new colophon.
- **No per-fixture tabtargets for calibrant family** — framework-test plumbing, not operator workflows. The bash testbench invokes `rbtd` directly; deviates from BBAAe's per-fixture pattern, justified because pristine / canonical are operator-facing and calibrant isn't.
- **BURV chain is contract, not hook** — `BURV_TEMP_ROOT_DIR` / `BURV_OUTPUT_ROOT_DIR` (BUS0 §540-552) is load-bearing infrastructure. Calibrant cases nest BURV through `rbtd` invocations and incidentally regression-test the chain.
- **Manifest-coverage check enforcement (BCAAB):**
  - Positive at all three `rbtdri_invoke_*` primitives — refuses undeclared invocations
  - Negative in `run_suite` after `run_sections` returns success — gated on `result.failed == 0` (failure paths suppress)
  - `run_single` enforces positive only — cannot satisfy exhaustiveness by construction

## Out of scope

- Cross-kit testbench composition (BUK fixtures registered in rbk testbench)
- Orchestrating tabtargets containing exec-of-children logic
- Trace-file format invariants beyond a single `pass_with_output` smoke check
- Color rendering / terminal width contracts
- Imprint coverage (which nameplate a per-imprint colophon was called with), argument patterns, compile-time / static analysis
- Fixture-level declaration edits in existing fixtures (canonical-establish, etc.) — surface as separate finds if BCAAB's negative check turns up stale entries during ₣BB's gauntlet run

## References

- ₣BB paddock § "Calibrant fixture family — BBAAh" — anchor-word rationale, module-rename decision, Rust fixture catalog
- ₣BB BBAAh — calibrant fixture foundation (prerequisite)
- BBAAd — engine concepts (`rbtdre_Disposition`, `rbtdre_resolve_fail_fast`, `rbtdrb_Probe`)
- `rbtdri_invoke`, `rbtdri_invoke_global`, `rbtdri_invoke_imprint` — chokepoint primitives BCAAB modifies
- `Tools/rbk/rbts/` — case-file convention (BCAAA establishes; BCAAD extends)
- BUS0 §540-552 — BURV chain contract

# jjezs_pace ₢BCAAA

Drafted from ₢BBAAi in ₣BB.

## Character

BUK-side testbench wiring that black-box-drives the calibrant fixtures (Pace BBAAh) through the `rbtd` binary. Asserts CLI exit codes, stderr diagnostic format, fail-fast policy across fixture and suite layers, and the disposition × keep-going gate landed in BBAAd. The bash layer covers what Rust unit tests cannot honestly self-test: the binary's operator-facing surface and the bash bridge's reaction to verdicts.

Intent-only docket. Section/case catalog locked in paddock — see "Bash blackbox calibrant — design." Case organization, assertion helpers, exact zipper colophon, and tabtarget naming chosen at mount time.

## Scope

- New module `Tools/rbk/rbtt_testbench.sh` registering 14 cases across 6 sections per paddock catalog.
- Six case files in `Tools/rbk/rbts/rbtc*_*.sh` — one per section.
- Zipper entry in `rbz_zipper.sh` mapping new colophon → testbench module.
- One tabtarget stub in `tt/` (one-line exec, with-the-grain).
- Release runbook entry documenting `buw-st` → new-colophon sequence.

## Out of scope

- Cross-kit composition (BUK fixtures registered in rbk testbench).
- Orchestrating tabtargets that exec multiple children.
- Explicit BURV-chain regression tests (incidentally exercised; no dedicated cases).

## Depends on

Pace BBAAh (calibrant-fixture-foundation) — needs the calibrant fixtures registered in the manifest and the `calibrant` suite available before bash assertions can pass.

# jjezs_pace ₢BCAAB

Drafted from ₢BBAAj in ₣BB.

## Character

Tighten rbtdrm's per-fixture required-colophons list from "asserted superset" to "exhaustive subset". Two runtime checks, both at the rbtdri_invoke_* primitive chokepoint:

- Positive: every invoked colophon must be in the fixture's declared list — refuse otherwise.
- Negative: every declared colophon must have been invoked at least once on a successful full-fixture run — fail otherwise.

Surgical refactor; no case-body edits expected. The audit risk for the negative check surfaces at turn-on; existing TestSuite runs serve as the audit gate.

## Scope

- rbtdri_Context tracks declared and used colophon sets
- All three invoke primitives (rbtdri_invoke, _global, _imprint) refuse undeclared invocations and record successful ones
- run_suite enforces the negative check after run_sections returns success (gated on result.failed == 0 — failure paths suppress the check)
- run_single enforces positive only — single-case cannot satisfy exhaustiveness by construction
- Unit tests covering both check directions and the single-case exemption
- Operator-facing diagnostics name the fixture and the offending colophon

## Cross-pace dependencies

None. The check applies to all theurge fixtures, independent of gauntlet assembly.

## Out of scope

- Imprint coverage (which nameplate a per-imprint colophon was called with), argument patterns, compile-time/static analysis
- Phase-split into two paces — implement both checks together; the TestSuite run is the validation gate
- Any fixture-level declaration edits — the implementation should pass clean against current declarations or surface a real stale entry as a separate find

# jjezs_pace ₢BCAAC

## Character

Rust extension to the calibrant family proving BCAAB's runtime manifest-coverage checks land their diagnostics. Three synthetic fixtures with deliberately aligned or mis-declared manifests; one synthetic theurge-internal noop tabtarget the fixtures invoke without cloud or filesystem side-effects. Self-contained — bash driver pace consumes this but does not land here.

Intent-only docket. Catalog locked below; module placement, tabtarget naming, manifest constant naming chosen at mount time.

## Scope

- Synthetic noop tabtarget, theurge-internal (rbtd-l* family — parity with rbtdrl_calibrant.rs).
- Three new fixtures in the calibrant family (Independent disposition):
  - calibrant-coverage-aligned: manifest declares noop, case invokes noop → Pass.
  - calibrant-coverage-undeclared: manifest declares nothing, case invokes noop → fixture-level FAIL via positive check.
  - calibrant-coverage-unused: manifest declares noop, case invokes nothing → fixture-level FAIL via negative check.
- Manifest entries + disposition tags.
- Rust unit tests pin only registration shape; verdict assertions live in the bash driver pace.

## Depends on

- ₣BB BBAAh (calibrant-fixture-foundation) — needs rbtdrl_calibrant.rs and the calibrant-family scaffolding.
- BCAAB (theurge-manifest-coverage-check) — without the runtime checks landed, the failing fixtures pass instead of fail.

## Out of scope

- Bash assertions on exit code or stderr shape — sibling pace.
- Inclusion in the calibrant suite — these fixtures are standalone-invoked so the existing suite-fail-fast assertions remain clean.
- Cross-kit composition.

# jjezs_pace ₢BCAAD

## Character

BUK-side testbench extension that black-box-drives the three coverage fixtures from the sibling Rust pace (BCAAC) through the rbtd binary. Asserts CLI exit codes and stderr diagnostic shapes for the positive check (invoked-not-declared) and the negative check (declared-not-invoked), plus the single-case exemption for the negative check.

Intent-only docket. Section and case catalog below; case-file naming and assertion helpers chosen at mount time.

## Scope

- New section manifest-coverage in rbtt_testbench.sh, four cases:
  - coverage-aligned-passes — Run.calibrant-coverage-aligned exits 0.
  - coverage-undeclared-fails — Run.calibrant-coverage-undeclared exits non-zero; stderr matches the positive-check diagnostic naming the offending colophon.
  - coverage-unused-fails — Run.calibrant-coverage-unused exits non-zero; stderr names the unused colophon.
  - coverage-single-case-skips-negative — SingleCase invocation of calibrant-coverage-unused's case exits 0 (proves the single-case exemption).
- Case files in Tools/rbk/rbts/ following BCAAA convention.
- Tabtarget(s) where manifest-driven generation yields them; testbench colophon registration.

## Depends on

1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BG
2	
3	## Goal
4	
5	Normalize and upgrade existing `.adoc` orchestration subdocuments to
6	the orchestration-spec discipline that emerged from the style-guide
7	design under heat ₣A-. The pattern was developed empirically against
8	`BUSJGW-GarrisonWsl.adoc` (Windows workload garrison via WSL) and is
9	captured in
10	`Memos/memo-20260511-orchestration-style-axla-draft.md`.
11	
12	The exemplar that grounds this heat is the current state of BUSJGW:
13	explicit `{busc_*}` / `{rbbc_*}` control verbs in every step body,
14	FATAL recovery-scope discipline, trailing Rationale section
15	(load-bearing only), trailing References section (epistemic
16	provenance), step granularity matching the operation's transport
17	contract (WSG WSp-105 for Windows; REST atomicity for cloud), no
18	false probe-then-conditional branches when one idempotent dispatch
19	suffices.
20	
21	## Locked Decisions
22	
23	- **Concrete-first codification.** Stress-test the discipline on a
24	  second exemplar (`RBSAC-ark_conjure`, Cloud Build / REST
25	  transport) before committing the AXLA dimension. Memo notes this
26	  explicitly.
27	- **Codify early, not late.** Once the dimension shape survives
28	  both BUSJGW (Windows) and RBSAC (cloud REST), commit AXLA
29	  immediately. Late codification leaves remaining scrubs
30	  referencing an uncommitted memo -- the partial-products failure
31	  mode.
32	- **Proposed dimension name:** `axd_orchestrating`. Alternatives in
33	  the memo: `axd_composing`, `axd_compositional`. Final decision at
34	  the codification pace.
35	- **False-branches anti-pattern is load-bearing.** A failed step
36	  with a unique die string (`buc_die` / `rbbc_fatal` /
37	  `busc_fatal`) is sufficient diagnostic. Reserve real branching
38	  for dynamic discovery + iteration, or where the probe result is
39	  consumed by something other than the dispatch.
40	- **Runtime variables declared in sequence via control verbs.** No
41	  separate Runtime Values table.
42	
43	## Candidate Orchestrations
44	
45	Density signal: `axhob_operation` + `axhos_step` count + control-verb
46	count + `_fatal` count. Regenerate via grep over
47	`Tools/buk/vov_veiled/*.adoc` and `Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/*.adoc`
48	filtering for `axhob_operation`.
49	
50	**Tier 1 -- Windows posture family** (direct siblings of BUSJGW;
51	lockstep treatment):
52	
53	- `BUSJCW-CaparisonWindows` -- 14 steps / 12 calls / 14 fatals
54	- `BUSJIW-InvigilateWindows` -- 8 / 8 / 8

file.content:
# jjezs_paddock ₣BG

## Goal

Normalize and upgrade existing `.adoc` orchestration subdocuments to
the orchestration-spec discipline that emerged from the style-guide
design under heat ₣A-. The pattern was developed empirically against
`BUSJGW-GarrisonWsl.adoc` (Windows workload garrison via WSL) and is
captured in
`Memos/memo-20260511-orchestration-style-axla-draft.md`.

The exemplar that grounds this heat is the current state of BUSJGW:
explicit `{busc_*}` / `{rbbc_*}` control verbs in every step body,
FATAL recovery-scope discipline, trailing Rationale section
(load-bearing only), trailing References section (epistemic
provenance), step granularity matching the operation's transport
contract (WSG WSp-105 for Windows; REST atomicity for cloud), no
false probe-then-conditional branches when one idempotent dispatch
suffices.

## Locked Decisions

- **Concrete-first codification.** Stress-test the discipline on a
  second exemplar (`RBSAC-ark_conjure`, Cloud Build / REST
  transport) before committing the AXLA dimension. Memo notes this
  explicitly.
- **Codify early, not late.** Once the dimension shape survives
  both BUSJGW (Windows) and RBSAC (cloud REST), commit AXLA
  immediately. Late codification leaves remaining scrubs
  referencing an uncommitted memo -- the partial-products failure
  mode.
- **Proposed dimension name:** `axd_orchestrating`. Alternatives in
  the memo: `axd_composing`, `axd_compositional`. Final decision at
  the codification pace.
- **False-branches anti-pattern is load-bearing.** A failed step
  with a unique die string (`buc_die` / `rbbc_fatal` /
  `busc_fatal`) is sufficient diagnostic. Reserve real branching
  for dynamic discovery + iteration, or where the probe result is
  consumed by something other than the dispatch.
- **Runtime variables declared in sequence via control verbs.** No
  separate Runtime Values table.

## Candidate Orchestrations

Density signal: `axhob_operation` + `axhos_step` count + control-verb
count + `_fatal` count. Regenerate via grep over
`Tools/buk/vov_veiled/*.adoc` and `Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/*.adoc`
filtering for `axhob_operation`.

**Tier 1 -- Windows posture family** (direct siblings of BUSJGW;
lockstep treatment):

- `BUSJCW-CaparisonWindows` -- 14 steps / 12 calls / 14 fatals
- `BUSJIW-InvigilateWindows` -- 8 / 8 / 8
- `BUSJGC-GarrisonCygwin` -- 7 / 5 / 7
- `BUSJGB-GarrisonBash` -- 7 / 0 / 0 (needs full verb pass)

**Tier 2 -- Cross-platform mirrors:**

- `BUSJCM`, `BUSJCL` -- 5 / 5 / 5 each
- `BUSJIM`, `BUSJIL` -- 8 / 8 / 8 each

**Tier 3 -- High-density GCP orchestrations** (REST transport;
several have zero fatals despite being core composition):

- `RBSDE-depot_levy` -- 14 / 11 / 1
- `RBSDK-director_invest` -- 12 / 9 / 2
- `RBSDU-depot_unmake` -- 8 / 10 / 5
- `RBSGM-governor_mantle` -- 8 / 7 / 0 (no fatals -- major scrub)
- `RBSRK-retriever_invest` -- 8 / 6 / 2
- `RBSTB-trigger_build` -- 7 / 3 / 6 (empirical retry/poll lives
  here)
- `RBSAC-ark_conjure` -- 5 / 1 / 0 (stress-test candidate; core
  Cloud Build orchestration with no fatals)
- `RBSAV-ark_vouch` -- 8 / 7 / 1
- `RBSAB-ark_about` -- 4 / 6 / 0
- `RBSAA-ark_abjure` -- 5 / 2 / 5
- `RBSAG-ark_graft` -- 5 / 5 / 0
- `RBSAS-ark_summon` -- 5 / 3 / 1

**Tier 4 -- Probably not orchestrations** (review to confirm or
document discriminator):

- Handbooks: `BUSJHW`, `BUSJHM`, `BUSJHL`
- Regime list/render/validate: `BUSTLL`, `BUSTPL`, `BUSTLR`,
  `BUSTPR`, `BUSTLV`, `BUSTPV`
- Low-level wrappers: `BUSJWC`, `BUSJWS`, `BUSJWK`, `BUSJPS`,
  `RBSCE`
- Display/inventory ops: `RBSDL`, `RBSCL`, `RBSAP`

## Suggested Pace Order (Cut When Heat Engages)

1. RBSAC stress-test scrub (Cloud Build / REST transport)
2. AXLA codification: mint dimension, lint contract, false-branches
   anti-pattern; voice on BUSJGW and RBSAC definition sites
3. Tier 1 (BUSJCW, BUSJIW, BUSJGC, BUSJGB)
4. Tier 2 (cross-platform mirrors)
5. Tier 3 (GCP orchestrations)
6. Tier 4 review (confirm non-orchestration; document
   discriminator)

Steps 1 and 2 gate the rest: the dimension must survive both
transports before codification; remaining scrubs reference the
codified dimension.

## What Done Looks Like

- Every orchestrating procedure in `Tools/buk/vov_veiled/` and
  `Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/` voices the codified dimension at its
  definition site.
- Each conforms to the lint contract: control verb per step,
  trailing Rationale and References sections, FATAL recovery-scope
  discipline, false-branches anti-pattern applied.
- A simple prose ↔ control-verb consistency lint exists (regex
  pass, per the memo).
- Tier 4 specs either get the dimension or carry explicit
  documentation of why they don't.

## Notes

- The memo
  `Memos/memo-20260511-orchestration-style-axla-draft.md` is the
  working spec for the discipline. Read it first when mounting any
  pace.
- The BUSJGW exemplar at
  `Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BUSJGW-GarrisonWsl.adoc` is the worked
  reference.
- LLM failure modes to corral against (self-flagged):
  over-engineering retry/backoff trees, reintroducing rationale
  into step bodies, adding "improved" error handling not in the
  source spec, generating cross-references that break silently.
  Defer to the codified discipline; do not elaborate beyond it.

Heat: rbk-22-win-tadmor-deepening (₣BI)
Status: stabled
Created: 260512


## Paces

### scry-sentry-interface-coverage (₢BIAAA) [rough]

Drafted from ₢A_AAe in ₣A_.

## Character

Tooling consideration — small change, clear motivation.

## Docket

Decide how to broaden scry's sentry-side capture beyond the hardcoded
`-i eth1`. On linux Docker Engine, port-publish traffic delivered to
sentry's enclave bridge arrives on the enclave interface; scry's
current capture misses it entirely. Pentacle's capture is unaffected
(single interface).

Options to weigh: `-i any` on sentry, parameterize the interface,
or mirror rbjs_sentry.sh's IP-based discovery.

### References

- `Tools/rbk/rboo_observe.sh` — `rboo_observe()` and the sentry exec.

### scry-charge-aware-lifecycle (₢BIAAB) [rough]

Drafted from ₢A_AAf in ₣A_.

## Character

Tooling consideration — workflow gap.

## Docket

Decide whether scry should tolerate the case where target containers
don't exist yet at launch. Today scry's `docker exec tcpdump` children
fail fast when containers are absent, the launcher's `wait` returns,
and scry exits cleanly without ever capturing — making the workflow
"start scry, then charge in another shell" impossible.

Options: poll-and-wait for containers with a timeout (default true),
explicit `--await` flag (opt-in), or no change (document the ordering
requirement).

### scry-finite-duration-exit (₢BIAAC) [rough]

Drafted from ₢A_AAg in ₣A_.

## Character

Tooling consideration — agentic composability.

## Docket

Decide whether scry should support a bounded-run mode. Today scry
runs until Ctrl+C; agentic and scripted callers need a `--duration N`
(seconds) or `--packets N` (per-stream count) so the captured window
is finite and `wait` returns naturally without signal handling.

Implementation: propagate the bound to each tcpdump child via
`timeout N` or `tcpdump -c N`. Default = current unbounded behavior.

### scry-filter-argument (₢BIAAD) [rough]

Drafted from ₢A_AAh in ₣A_.

## Character

Tooling consideration — signal-to-noise.

## Docket

Decide whether scry should accept an optional positional tcpdump filter
expression. Today scry runs unfiltered captures; output is dominated by
routine mDNS (5353), ICMPv6 router solicitations, and ARP chatter, and
the interesting traffic gets buried. With a filter arg the targeted
form becomes `tt/rbw-cs.Scry.sh srjcl 'tcp port 7999 or tcp port 8000'`.

Default = no filter (current behavior). Quoting/escaping of the
expression across the BUK dispatch layer needs verification.

### scry-docker-bridge-capture (₢BIAAE) [rough]

Drafted from ₢A_AAi in ₣A_.

## Character

Tooling consideration — coverage extension.

## Docket

Decide whether scry's host-side bridge capture should extend to the
docker runtime. Today the bridge capture is gated on
`RBRN_RUNTIME=podman` and traverses `podman machine ssh`; on linux
Docker Engine the bridge interface is host-visible (no VM in the
path), so a `sudo tcpdump -i <bridge_iface>` could give a third
correlation point — host arrival vs container arrival — that is
currently a blind spot for cross-platform investigation.

Discovery of the bridge interface for a docker network is via
`docker network inspect <name> --format '{{.NetworkInterface}}'`,
mirroring the existing podman path.

### scry-plain-output-mode (₢BIAAF) [rough]

Drafted from ₢A_AAj in ₣A_.

## Character

Tooling consideration — minor convenience.

## Docket

Decide whether scry should support a `--plain` output mode that omits
ANSI color codes from its line prefixes. Today every emitted line
carries embedded escape sequences (`\033[37m\033[1m[SENTRY]\033[0m`)
which complicate grep/awk processing for programmatic consumers.
Default = current colored output for terminal use.

### ifrit-sortie-dnat-source-observation (₢BIAAG) [rough]

Drafted from ₢A_AAk in ₣A_.

## Character

In-bottle empirical regression backstop for POSTROUTING MASQUERADE return-path symmetry. Currently only cerebro validates this at iptables-counter level; a silent regression of the MASQUERADE clause would pass every existing tadmor case while breaking entry-port flow.

## Docket

Add a coordinated sortie: ifrit binds a TCP listener on the bottle's enclave-side entry port (`RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_ENCLAVE`), theurge initiates an external TCP connect to sentry's published port (`RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_WORKSTATION`), ifrit accepts and reads peer-source via `getpeername`.

Verdict: PASS iff observed source IP equals `RBRN_ENCLAVE_SENTRY_IP`. Real external client IP indicates MASQUERADE didn't fire (asymmetric return path, architecture broken). Any other value indicates unknown deviation.

### References

- `rbev-vessels/common-ifrit-context/src/rbida_sorties.rs` — sortie body site; theurge-observes-ifrit-effect patterns near `sortie_arp_send_gratuitous` (role reversed here: theurge sends, ifrit observes).
- `rbev-vessels/common-ifrit-context/src/rbida_attacks.rs` — adversary registration.
- `Memos/memo-20260512-sentry-entry-port-repair-anchor.md` — MASQUERADE rationale.
- Pace ₢A_AAb — architecture this validates from the receiver side.

### ifrit-sortie-spoof-from-outside-as-enclave-ip (₢BIAAH) [rough]

Drafted from ₢A_AAl in ₣A_.

## Character

Deferred — needs host-side raw-socket sending, not in-bottle ifrit invocation. Different harness surface from the rest of the sortie family. Mount only if sentry-local services grow beyond dnsmasq, or a specific spoof-from-outside concern surfaces.

## Docket

Test the inbound mirror of ₢A_AAc: host-side attacker spoofs source as `RBRN_ENCLAVE_SENTRY_IP` or `RBRN_ENCLAVE_BOTTLE_IP`, aimed at sentry's external IP at the published entry port. PREROUTING per-IP RETURN short-circuits DNAT — packet is locally received by sentry instead. Verify no sentry-local service accepts under those spoofed sources.

Why low-priority now: sentry-local surface is dnsmasq on enclave-IP:53 only; existing `sortie_sentry_udp_non_dns` covers the realistic concern. The gap is theoretical until the sentry grows other listeners. Architectural watchpoint: any new sentry-local service is a trigger to mount.

### References

- `Memos/memo-20260512-sentry-entry-port-repair-anchor.md` — per-IP RETURN architecture creates this mirror gap.
- `rbev-vessels/common-ifrit-context/src/rbida_sorties.rs` — `sortie_direct_sentry_probe` (defensive sentry-port baseline) and `sortie_sentry_udp_non_dns`.
- Pace ₢A_AAc — outbound mirror of this attack class.

### consolidate-srcip-spoof-parameterization (₢BIAAI) [rough]

Drafted from ₢A_AAm in ₣A_.

## Character

Refactor decision. Mount-time choice between folding ₢A_AAc's external-spoof into the existing `sortie_net_srcip_spoof` body or keeping the two sorties separate. Mount only after ₢A_AAc lands.

## Docket

`sortie_net_srcip_spoof` in `rbev-vessels/common-ifrit-context/src/rbida_sorties.rs` has a `spoof_tests` table with three rows: spoof-as-sentry, spoof-as-allowed-cidr, spoof-as-loopback. ₢A_AAc adds a fourth class: spoof-as-external-routable-IP (`192.168.65.1`, and possibly `169.254.169.254`, `0.0.0.0`, `224.0.0.1`).

Decision:
- **Fold in**: extend the existing table, single adversary entry, smaller maintenance surface.
- **Keep separate**: preserve docket-level distinction between spoof-as-known-actor and spoof-as-arbitrary-routable; two adversary entries, two selectors.

If folding: drop the separate sortie + adversary registration, update tadmor fixture's expected adversary set, rename the consolidated case if the new scope warrants it.

### References

- `rbev-vessels/common-ifrit-context/src/rbida_sorties.rs` — `sortie_net_srcip_spoof` and its `spoof_tests` table.
- `rbev-vessels/common-ifrit-context/src/rbida_attacks.rs` — adversary registration site.
- Pace ₢A_AAc — the new sortie subject to consolidation.

File-touch bitmap: (no work file changes)

# jjezs_paddock ₣BI

## Context

Two threads deepening tadmor's adversarial coverage of sentry containment, paired with the tooling lift required to produce trustworthy evidence:

1. **Scry revival and improvement** — `rbw-cs.Scry` is the network-traffic observation tool used during tadmor runs to verify what an attacker's traffic actually reaches. The tool exists but has gaps that force operators into ad-hoc workarounds. Six paces extend it: sentry-interface coverage, charge-aware lifecycle, finite-duration exit (composability), filter argument (signal-to-noise), docker-bridge capture (coverage extension), and plain-output mode (scriptable consumption).

2. **New tadmor cases** — three adversarial cases extending the existing tadmor-security fixture: an in-bottle empirical regression backstop for POSTROUTING MASQUERADE source-IP behavior, a deferred outside-as-enclave-IP spoof (needs host-side raw-socket sending, not in-bottle ifrit posture), and a consolidation of source-IP spoof parameterization across the existing and new cases.

## Scope and boundaries

- **Test-side and tool-side only.** Sentry hardening itself — the entry-port DNAT-forward rescope, related spec deltas, sentry-config presence assertions — stays in `₣A_`. That hardening work is mid-flight there with code already landed; this heat consumes the new sentry behavior in tests, it doesn't drive it.
- **Scry tool changes are non-breaking.** Plain-output and filter-argument are additive ergonomics; charge-aware lifecycle and finite-duration exit make the tool usable from automation. The existing interactive invocation path keeps working.
- **One deferral is locked.** The outside-as-enclave-IP spoof case is intentionally not implementable in-bottle — it needs a host-side raw-socket sender. The pace records the deferral and the reason; mounting it produces a host-side stub or a stronger deferral, not an in-bottle attempt.

## Done

Scry is comfortable to drive from automation (filter, plain output, finite-duration exit, charge-aware lifecycle all wired). The new tadmor cases land green in the tadmor-security fixture without flake. Source-IP spoof parameterization is consolidated across the cases (or the consolidation pace is explicitly declined on shape grounds at mount).

## Cross-heat reference

The source-IP-spoof consolidation pace references prior ifrit-sortie work that landed in `₣A_` (enclave-spoof-external). That work is complete and stable; the reference is for context, not dependency.

# jjezs_pace ₢BIAAA

Drafted from ₢A_AAe in ₣A_.

## Character

Tooling consideration — small change, clear motivation.

## Docket

Decide how to broaden scry's sentry-side capture beyond the hardcoded
`-i eth1`. On linux Docker Engine, port-publish traffic delivered to
sentry's enclave bridge arrives on the enclave interface; scry's
current capture misses it entirely. Pentacle's capture is unaffected
(single interface).

Options to weigh: `-i any` on sentry, parameterize the interface,
or mirror rbjs_sentry.sh's IP-based discovery.

### References

- `Tools/rbk/rboo_observe.sh` — `rboo_observe()` and the sentry exec.

# jjezs_pace ₢BIAAB

Drafted from ₢A_AAf in ₣A_.

## Character

Tooling consideration — workflow gap.

## Docket

Decide whether scry should tolerate the case where target containers
don't exist yet at launch. Today scry's `docker exec tcpdump` children
fail fast when containers are absent, the launcher's `wait` returns,

content.text:
I've pulled all five paddocks and their dockets. Here's the groom — and an important framing note up front.

## The naming is misleading — only one of these is actually Windows

All five silks carry `win`, but the content says otherwise. Only **₣A-** is literally Windows-host work. The `rbk-20/21/22-win-*` heats are RB test-stack infrastructure that got swept into the same numbered series — they're prerequisites/quality work for the broader test apparatus, not Windows-reach work:

| Firemark | Silks | Progress | What it's *actually* about | Windows? |
|----------|-------|----------|---------------------------|----------|
| **₣A-** | rbk-30-win-windows-remote-control | **40/45** | Jurisdiction machinery to reach/garrison a Windows host | **Yes — the spine** |
| ₣BG | rbk-22-win-orchestration-scrub | 0/0 | `.adoc` orchestration-style discipline scrub (all docs) | Adjacent (born from BUSJGW) |
| ₣BI | rbk-22-win-tadmor-deepening | 0/9 | Scry tool + 3 tadmor container-security cases | No |
| ₣BC | rbk-21-win-theurge-tabarget-auditing | 0/4 | Theurge framework self-test (calibrant fixtures) | No |
| ₣BN | rbk-20-win-depot-regime | 0/0 | RBRD depot-immutable settings + GCS tripwire | No |

All five are **stabled**.

## Where Windows testing actually stands: ₣A- is nearly converged

**Goal:** run RB's container isolation tests on a Windows host (`bujn-winpc`) via WSL + Cygwin + PowerShell paths, Docker Desktop as the release-1 runtime. The jurisdiction architecture (BURN/BURP regimes; Caparison/Invigilate/Garrison verbs) is **built and landed** — 40 of 45 paces done, last 11 commits all on the live-debug pace. Five rough paces remain:

1. **₢A-ABG** (windows-setup-first-time-debug) — *the live convergence pace.* Interactive, no bounded cycle count; caparison-windows + garrison-w + workload round-trip on `bujn-winpc`, spec and code agreeing at close. This is the active front — where the last 11 commits landed. **Done = operator declares convergence.**
2. **₢A-ABJ** (revert-wsl-stage-dev-cache) — promote the DEV CACHE shape to formal "admin-no-WSL" architecture (admin produces seed tar, never registers `rbtww-main` in its own HKCU\Lxss; garrison-w imports from the cache path).
3. **₢A-ABK** (windows-reboot-primitive-spec-coherence) — bring BUSJGW/BUSJCW/WSG specs into line with the reboot-as-precondition + admin-no-WSL shapes landed in ABG/ABJ. Spec-coherence only.
4. **₢A-AAx** (practice-windows-jurisdiction-end-to-end) — operator-driven full ceremony: WSL path, then Cygwin path, then the Cygwin split-HOME probe decision. Human-driven, agent amends on friction.
5. **₢A-AA5** (bash-spec-link-comments) — comment-only cleanup binding bash symbols to spec sections. Trailing.

The critical path is **ABG** (live debug) → its architecture spawns **ABJ** + **ABK** → **AAx** validates end-to-end → **AA5** cleans up. The standing notes flag two open items folding into ABG/ABJ scope: the `WSL_UTF8=1` UTF-16LE stdout drop (→ ABG) and `C:\Users\bujuw_user.*` orphan wildcard cleanup (→ ABJ). Required mount-time reading is `Memos/memo-20260516-windows-headless-account-anatomy.md`.

## The other four (none blocking Windows reach)

- **₣BI** (0/9) — fully sloted: 6 scry-tool improvements (interface coverage, charge-aware lifecycle, finite duration, filter arg, docker-bridge capture, plain output) + 3 tadmor cases (MASQUERADE return-path backstop, deferred outside-as-enclave spoof, srcip-spoof consolidation). Test-side/tool-side only; sentry hardening itself stays in ₣A_. Ready to mount, no dependencies blocking.
- **₣BC** (0/4) — theurge self-test: bash blackbox driver + manifest-coverage check + coverage fixtures + coverage driver. **Gated on ₣BB's BBAAh** (calibrant fixture foundation) landing first.
- **₣BG** (0/0) — orchestration `.adoc` scrub. No paces sloted; paddock has a full candidate list and suggested order (RBSAC stress-test → AXLA codification → tiers). Needs slating before it can run.
- **₣BN** (0/0) — RBRD depot regime + tripwire. No paces sloted; one mount-time decision still open (manifest substrate: GCS object vs `rbi_df` GAR artifacts).

## Bottom line

Windows testing-host enablement is **one heat (₣A-), nearly done** — the architecture is landed and you're in interactive convergence on `bujn-winpc`. The realistic next action to "establish Windows testing" is to resume **₢A-ABG** on the live host (or run the **₢A-AAx** end-to-end practice if ABG has already converged). The other four heats harden the test stack but don't gate getting tests running on Windows.

Want me to mount **₣A-** (orient on ABG to see exactly where the live debug left off), or would you rather I dig into any one of these in more detail first?

180	## Noninteractive forced-command variants
181	
182	The brad@rocket worked example above puts the account on an interactive
183	Cygwin shell — useful for ad-hoc human work, less useful for programmatic
184	access where each SSH connection should run a single command and exit.
185	The same chain (account creation, `__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/<name>/`
186	authkeys, ACL lockdown, Match block) also supports noninteractive
187	substrate-routing personas where the forced-command field encodes which
188	substrate the session lands in. Two variants were stood up alongside
189	brad@rocket on 2026-05-17 and verified:
190	
191	- `cygwin@rocket` — `ssh cygwin@rocket "uname -o"` → `Cygwin`
192	- `wsl@rocket` — `ssh wsl@rocket "uname -o"` → `GNU/Linux`
193	
194	The differences from brad@rocket fall in three places.
195	
196	### Forced-command pattern
197	
198	Brad's forced-command launches an interactive shell:
199	
200	```
201	command="C:\cygwin64\bin\bash --login -i"
202	```
203	
204	The noninteractive variants intercept the SSH-protocol-level original
205	command (which sshd exposes as the `SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` environment
206	variable in the spawned process) and route it through the substrate.
207	
208	**cygwin@rocket.** The forced-command launches a non-interactive Cygwin
209	login shell and feeds it `$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` via `-c`:
210	
211	```
212	command="C:\cygwin64\bin\bash --login -c \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\""
213	```
214	
215	The `\"` is sshd's authkeys-string escape for `"`; after parsing, the
216	inner command is `bash --login -c "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"`. When sshd
217	spawns the forced-command process, the `SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` env var
218	is set on the child. Bash expands the variable inside the `-c` script
219	body and executes whatever the client supplied.
220	
221	**wsl@rocket.** Embedding the wsl.exe invocation directly in the
222	authkeys command= field works in principle but compounds escape layers
223	(the inner `$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` must survive sshd's authkeys parsing,
224	cmd.exe's interpretation, and wsl.exe's argument splitting). A wrapper
225	`.cmd` file at `C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\wsl\runcmd.cmd` isolates the
226	runtime concern:
227	
228	```
229	@echo off
230	set WSLENV=SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND/u
231	C:\Windows\System32\wsl.exe -d Ubuntu-24.04 -u root -- bash -lc "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"
232	```
233	
234	The authkeys forced-command then becomes:
235	
236	```
237	command="C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\wsl\runcmd.cmd"
238	```
239

file.content:
## Noninteractive forced-command variants

The brad@rocket worked example above puts the account on an interactive
Cygwin shell — useful for ad-hoc human work, less useful for programmatic
access where each SSH connection should run a single command and exit.
The same chain (account creation, `__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/<name>/`
authkeys, ACL lockdown, Match block) also supports noninteractive
substrate-routing personas where the forced-command field encodes which
substrate the session lands in. Two variants were stood up alongside
brad@rocket on 2026-05-17 and verified:

- `cygwin@rocket` — `ssh cygwin@rocket "uname -o"` → `Cygwin`
- `wsl@rocket` — `ssh wsl@rocket "uname -o"` → `GNU/Linux`

The differences from brad@rocket fall in three places.

### Forced-command pattern

Brad's forced-command launches an interactive shell:

```
command="C:\cygwin64\bin\bash --login -i"
```

The noninteractive variants intercept the SSH-protocol-level original
command (which sshd exposes as the `SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` environment
variable in the spawned process) and route it through the substrate.

**cygwin@rocket.** The forced-command launches a non-interactive Cygwin
login shell and feeds it `$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` via `-c`:

```
command="C:\cygwin64\bin\bash --login -c \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\""
```

The `\"` is sshd's authkeys-string escape for `"`; after parsing, the
inner command is `bash --login -c "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"`. When sshd
spawns the forced-command process, the `SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` env var
is set on the child. Bash expands the variable inside the `-c` script
body and executes whatever the client supplied.

**wsl@rocket.** Embedding the wsl.exe invocation directly in the
authkeys command= field works in principle but compounds escape layers
(the inner `$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` must survive sshd's authkeys parsing,
cmd.exe's interpretation, and wsl.exe's argument splitting). A wrapper
`.cmd` file at `C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\wsl\runcmd.cmd` isolates the
runtime concern:

```
@echo off
set WSLENV=SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND/u
C:\Windows\System32\wsl.exe -d Ubuntu-24.04 -u root -- bash -lc "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"
```

The authkeys forced-command then becomes:

```
command="C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\wsl\runcmd.cmd"
```

content.text:
Yes — I can reach the Windows box. The notes are in `Memos/memo-20260516-windows-headless-account-anatomy.md` (§"Noninteractive forced-command variants", lines 180+), which documents three ad-hoc SSH accounts stood up on `rocket` (tailnet hostname for `bujn-winpc`). All three are live right now:

| Account | Lands in | Verified | Use |
|---------|----------|----------|-----|
| `ssh brad@rocket` | Interactive Cygwin shell | ✅ connects, drops to `brad@rocket ~$` | Human ad-hoc; forced-command ignores any command you pass |
| `ssh cygwin@rocket "<cmd>"` | Non-interactive Cygwin | ✅ `uname -o` → `Cygwin` | Programmatic Cygwin path |
| `ssh wsl@rocket "<cmd>"` | WSL Ubuntu 24.04, as **root** | ✅ `uname -o` → `GNU/Linux`, Ubuntu 24.04.4 LTS | Programmatic WSL path |

**The notable find for Windows testing:** Docker is up and running inside WSL —

```
$ ssh wsl@rocket "docker --version; docker info --format '{{.ServerVersion}}'"
Docker version 29.1.3, build 29.1.3-0ubuntu3~24.04.2
29.1.3
```

So the container runtime that RB's isolation tests need is already live on the box through the `wsl@rocket` path. That's the substrate ₣A- has been building toward.

**Two caveats worth recording:**

1. **`cygwin@rocket` mangles multi-command strings.** `ssh cygwin@rocket "uname -o; whoami"` failed with `uname: unknown option -- ;` — the semicolons don't survive the sshd-authkeys → `bash --login -c` escape layers. Single commands are clean. The `wsl@rocket` wrapper (`runcmd.cmd` with `WSLENV`) handles multi-command strings fine, so `;`-joined bodies work there.
2. These three are the **ad-hoc hack accounts**, deliberately independent of the formal BURN/BURP caparison/garrison machinery that ₣A- (`tt/buw-jpS` / `tt/buw-jws bujn-winpc`) drives. Per CLAUDE.md they're "safe scratch for ad-hoc Windows testing" — not the production jurisdiction path.

Want me to probe further — e.g., run a quick RB tabtarget or `docker run hello-world` through `wsl@rocket` to confirm the test substrate end-to-end, or check whether the formal `tt/buw-jws bujn-winpc` workload path is also reachable?

for acct in cygwin wsl; do

  echo "--- [Q] quoting: printf '[%s]\\n' 'one two' ---"
  ssh -o ConnectTimeout=10 $acct@rocket "printf '[%s]\n' 'one two'" 2>&1
  echo "--- [V] var expand: X=5; echo val=\$X ---"
  ssh -o ConnectTimeout=10 $acct@rocket 'X=5; echo val=$X' 2>&1
  echo "--- [S] which shell binary is PID-self: readlink /proc/\$\$/exe (linux) or echo \$0 ---"
  ssh -o ConnectTimeout=10 $acct@rocket 'readlink /proc/$$/exe 2>/dev/null || echo "dollar0=$0"' 2>&1
  echo
done

content.text:
Now it's airtight. Here's what the testing actually shows — and yes, the `cygwin` identity's shell wiring **is** the issue.

## The two identities give fundamentally different shell contracts

| Probe | `wsl@rocket` | `cygwin@rocket` |
|-------|-------------|-----------------|
| `echo hello \| tr a-z A-Z` | `HELLO` ✅ | `hello \| tr a-z A-Z` ❌ (pipe literal) |
| `echo hi > file && cat file` | `hi` ✅ | `hi > /tmp/… && cat…` ❌ (redirect/`&&` literal) |
| `printf '[%s]\n' 'one two'` | `[one two]` ✅ | `'['one]` / `''[two']` ❌ (quotes not stripped) |
| `X=5; echo val=$X` | `val=5` ✅ | `bash: line 1: X=5;: command not found` ❌ |
| shell binary | `/usr/bin/bash` (Ubuntu bash 5.2, login) | Cygwin `/usr/bin/bash` — but see below |

## What's actually happening

Both accounts *do* reach a real bash (the `cygwin` error messages are literally `/usr/bin/bash: line 1: …`, so it's Cygwin bash, not cmd.exe — my first hypothesis was wrong). The difference is **how the command reaches that bash**:

- **`wsl@rocket`** hands your command to bash as a script that bash **parses normally** — `bash -lc '<your command>'`. Pipes, redirection, quoting, `;`, `$var`, `&&` all work. This is the `runcmd.cmd` + `WSLENV` wrapper documented in the memo (§"wsl@rocket"), and it's correct.

- **`cygwin@rocket`** hands your command to bash as a value that bash **expands but never re-parses**. The deployed forced-command (`command="C:\cygwin64\bin\bash --login -c \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\""`, memo §"cygwin@rocket") ends up running `bash -c '$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND'` where `$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` is expanded *during execution* and the result is only **word-split on whitespace** — never scanned for metacharacters. Net effect: you get exactly one bare command + space-split argv. No pipes, no `;`, no quoting, no `$var`, no redirection, no globs. That's why every earlier multi-part command broke (`uname -o; whoami` → uname saw `-o;` as an option).

So the symptom chain is: `cygwin@rocket` isn't giving you a degraded *shell* — it's giving you a **no-shell argv passthrough** wearing a bash error banner.

## Bottom line for Windows testing

- **`wsl@rocket` is the usable programmatic path right now** — full bash semantics, root in Ubuntu 24.04, Docker 29.1.3 daemon live. Everything RB's tests need runs through it.
- **`cygwin@rocket` is effectively broken for anything but a single bare command.** The fix is to make its forced-command match the wsl pattern (a wrapper that lets bash receive the command as a properly-parsed `-c` script, instead of an unparsed expanded variable). That's squarely the **Cygwin-path work in ₣A-** (pace ₢A-AAx exercises garrison-c, and the headless-account memo §"Forced-command pattern" is the spec home) — a future pace, not a now-fix.

Want me to draft the corrected cygwin forced-command and test it live against `rocket` (I have `brad@rocket` interactive access to edit the authkeys), or just record this finding into the ₣A- paddock / the headless memo as a characterized Pale-behavior note for when that pace mounts?

content: Goal: enable Windows testing on rocket. The wsl@rocket account works fully (bash, Docker live); cygwin@rocket only runs single bare commands because its shell wiring passes commands unparsed. Next: decide whether to fix cygwin's forced-command live or just record the finding. (disable recaps in /config)

=== burp.env (privileged/workload creds) — keys only, redact paths ===
BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER=bhyslop
BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE=/Users/bhyslop/.ssh/id_ed25519_winpc-admin
BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE=/Users/bhyslop/.ssh/id_ed25519_winpc-workload

=== bujb_privileged_ssh body ===
# bujb_privileged_ssh COMMAND... -- run an arbitrary command on the BURN
# node as BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER under key-only auth. Pass-through: argv is
# handed to ssh as the remote command without shell wrapping (operator
# prepends `powershell -Command`, `bash -c`, etc. as the platform requires).
# Caller must have invoked bujb_resolve_investiture beforehand. Returns
# ssh's exit code.
bujb_privileged_ssh() {
  zbujb_sentinel
  test "${ZBUJB_RESOLVED:-}" = "1" \
    || buc_die "bujb_privileged_ssh: call bujb_resolve_investiture first"
  test $# -ge 1 \
    || buc_die "bujb_privileged_ssh: command required"

  buc_step "Privileged SSH: ${BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER}@${BURN_HOST} (${BUZ_FOLIO})"

  local z_exit=0
  ssh -i "${BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE}"           \
      "${ZBUJB_SSH_BASE_ARGS[@]}"                \
      -o "${BUJB_ssh_opt_batchmode_yes}"         \
      -o "${BUJB_ssh_opt_connecttimeout_15}"     \
      "${BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER}@${BURN_HOST}"     \
      "$@"                                       \
    || z_exit=$?
  return "${z_exit}"
}

######################################################################
# Public: Invigilate verbs (BUSJI{W,M,L} — read-only host posture)
#
# Invigilate covers host-posture facts: registry/service/sleep/distribution
# state established by caparison or operator handbook. Workload-precondition
# probes (sudo NOPASSWD, admin-group) are a separate category and live in
# bujp_preflight — the locked BUSJI specs do not list them.

zbujb_invigilate_assert_platform() {
  zbujb_sentinel
  local -r z_required="${1:-}"
  test "${BURN_PLATFORM}" = "${z_required}" \
    || buc_die "invigilate requires ${z_required}, got '${BURN_PLATFORM}'"
}

# zbujb_invigilate_windows_op_facts -- the three operator-handbook
# preconditions that gate caparison-windows phase 3 (the WSL stage's
# multi-minute download). Shared between bujb_caparison_windows preflight
# (early-fail before WSL) and bujb_invigilate_windows (post-completion
# audit + standalone runs). Requires admin SSH already established under
# key-only auth.

content.text:
Confirmed — the broken local repro is character-identical to cygwin's live behavior, and the two-layer fix produces correct re-parsing. So I can answer both questions with grounding now.

## 1. The administrative command — yes, located and demonstrated live

**`tt/buw-jpS bujn-winpc <cmd>`** → runs as `rocket\bhyslop` (Windows admin), key-only auth via `~/.ssh/id_ed25519_winpc-admin`. I ran `whoami` through it → `rocket\bhyslop`. It's pass-through (you prepend `powershell -Command` / `bash -c` as needed; bare commands hit cmd.exe). This is the privileged path, and crucially it's the one that can **write** the ACL-locked `C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\…\authorized_keys` files — so it's also the repair lever.

## 2. Am I hopeful? Yes — strongly. The root cause is now pinned, not guessed.

Reading the two forced-commands side by side off the box made it obvious:

**`cygwin` (broken):**
```
command="C:\cygwin64\bin\bash --login -c \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\""
```
This line is launched by Windows OpenSSH's default shell, **cmd.exe**. But `$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` is *bash* syntax — cmd.exe uses `%VAR%` and leaves `$…` untouched. So bash's `-c` receives the literal token `$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND`, expands it once *during execution*, and the result is only **word-split — never re-parsed**. That's the whole bug: no pipes, no `;`, no quotes, no `$var`, no redirection. (Exactly the `bash -c '$SOC'` repro above.)

**`wsl` (working):**
```
command="C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\wsl\runcmd.cmd"
```
points at a wrapper that does the env-var→shell handoff correctly (`WSLENV` propagation + `wsl.exe … bash -lc`), so the command re-parses. The fix for cygwin is the *same architectural move*: a wrapper that adds one outer shell layer so the inner `bash -c` gets a fully-expanded, re-parseable script.

**Why I'm hopeful, concretely:**
- Root cause reproduced and understood (not "it's flaky").
- A correct sibling (`wsl`) already runs on the same box — proof the pattern is achievable here.
- The fix is small and low-risk: a Cygwin wrapper (`runcmd.sh`-style: `exec /bin/bash --login -c "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"`), parallel to `runcmd.cmd`.
- I have admin write access to deploy it **and** all three accounts to test it immediately after.
- Rollback is trivial — I've captured the current line verbatim, so reverting is one write.

**The honest caveats:** (a) editing a remote box's privileged SSH forced-command is a mutating, security-sensitive change, so I won't do it without your go-ahead; (b) my proposed Cygwin wrapper and wsl's `WSLENV` path are *different mechanisms* reaching the same end — I'd verify live rather than assume; (c) this is squarely ₣A-'s Cygwin-path territory (pace ₢A-AAx / the headless-account memo), so there's a real question of whether to fix ad-hoc now or fold it into that pace.

content.text:
Locked in. I'll do this in the WSG-robust way: transport everything via `powershell -EncodedCommand` (base64 of UTF-16LE) so there's **zero** cmd.exe/ssh quoting hazard, and write files with `WriteAllBytes` from base64 so the bytes (and LF line endings) are exact. I'll pre-flight the wrapper before touching `authorized_keys`, and I've captured the original line for instant rollback.

Let me generate the encoded payloads locally first.

input.command:
python3 - <<'PYEOF'
import base64, os

def enc(ps):  # powershell -EncodedCommand expects base64 of UTF-16LE
    return base64.b64encode(ps.encode('utf-16-le')).decode()

def b64(s):
    return base64.b64encode(s.encode()).decode()

PUBKEY = 'ssh-ed25519 AAAAC3NzaC1lZDI1NTE5AAAAIGz7bDP6GgZl1hvvhKJlPJUaP0f0eOvGotr+k2aare0u bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org'

# --- wrapper script (LF endings, trailing newline) ---
runcmd = '#!/bin/bash\nexec /bin/bash --login -c "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"\n'
B64_WRAPPER = b64(runcmd)

# --- new authorized_keys line (single backslashes in win path, like the working wsl line) ---
newkeys = 'command="C:\\cygwin64\\bin\\bash /cygdrive/c/ProgramData/ssh/users/cygwin/runcmd.sh" ' + PUBKEY + '\n'
B64_NEWKEYS = b64(newkeys)

# --- original authorized_keys line (for rollback) ---
origkeys = 'command="C:\\cygwin64\\bin\\bash --login -c \\"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\\"" ' + PUBKEY + '\n'
B64_ORIGKEYS = b64(origkeys)

WRAP_PATH = r'C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\cygwin\runcmd.sh'
KEYS_PATH = r'C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\cygwin\authorized_keys'

# PS: write wrapper
ps_writewrap = "[IO.File]::WriteAllBytes('%s',[Convert]::FromBase64String('%s'))" % (WRAP_PATH, B64_WRAPPER)
# PS: pre-flight test the wrapper exactly as sshd will invoke it
ps_test = ("$env:SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND='echo hi | tr a-z A-Z'; "
           "& 'C:\\cygwin64\\bin\\bash.exe' '/cygdrive/c/ProgramData/ssh/users/cygwin/runcmd.sh'")
# PS: write new authkeys
ps_writekeys = "[IO.File]::WriteAllBytes('%s',[Convert]::FromBase64String('%s'))" % (KEYS_PATH, B64_NEWKEYS)
# PS: restore original authkeys
ps_restore = "[IO.File]::WriteAllBytes('%s',[Convert]::FromBase64String('%s'))" % (KEYS_PATH, B64_ORIGKEYS)

out = {
  'enc_writewrap.txt': enc(ps_writewrap),
  'enc_test.txt':      enc(ps_test),
  'enc_writekeys.txt': enc(ps_writekeys),
  'enc_restore.txt':   enc(ps_restore),
}
for fn, val in out.items():
    with open('/tmp/'+fn, 'w') as f:
        f.write(val)

print("Wrapper script bytes:")
print(repr(runcmd))
print("\nNew authkeys line:")
print(repr(newkeys))
print("\nOriginal authkeys line (rollback):")
print(repr(origkeys))
print("\nEncoded payloads written to /tmp/enc_*.txt:")

content.text:
Done — and verified with a clean parity check. **`cygwin@rocket` now behaves identically to `wsl@rocket`:**

```
cygwin@rocket :  pipe=ABC var=7 host=rocket
wsl@rocket    :  pipe=ABC var=7 host=rocket
brad@rocket   :  unaffected (still interactive Cygwin)
```

Pipes, redirection, quoting, `$var`, arithmetic, and multi-statement `;` all work now — the original `uname -o; whoami` failure is gone.

## What the fix was

Three changes on the box, all via the `tt/buw-jpS` admin path, mirroring the working `wsl` sibling:

1. **New wrapper** `C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\cygwin\runcmd.sh` (LF endings):
   ```bash
   #!/bin/bash
   exec /bin/bash --login -c "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"
   ```
   The wrapper's bash is a *real* shell that expands `$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` and hands the result to an inner `bash -c`, which **re-parses** it. That's the missing second layer.
2. **Repointed `authorized_keys`** from the inline `bash --login -c "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"` (run by cmd.exe, which never expands `$…`) to `bash …/runcmd.sh`.
3. **ACL grant** `rocket\cygwin:(RX)` on the wrapper.

## The instructive gotcha (worth recording)

My pre-flight test passed but the real connection failed `Permission denied` — because the pre-flight ran as admin `bhyslop`, while the forced command runs as the unprivileged `cygwin` user, which couldn't even *read* the wrapper. The working `wsl\runcmd.cmd` carries `rocket\wsl:(RX)`; my `WriteAllBytes`-created file inherited only a transient logon SID. **Lesson for the Cygwin-path pace: a forced-command wrapper must grant `rocket\<user>:(RX)`, and you can't validate that as admin — you have to test through the target account.** This is exactly the kind of Pale-behavior the headless-account memo exists to capture.

## State

- All three ad-hoc accounts healthy; `wsl`/`brad` untouched.
- **Rollback** is still one command if ever needed — the original line is captured verbatim in this transcript: `command="C:\cygwin64\bin\bash --login -c \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\""`.
- No local repo changes were made (all edits were remote box config), so nothing to commit.

This was a live ad-hoc repair on the `cygwin`/`wsl`/`brad` scratch accounts — **not** the formal BURN/BURP garrison path. The formal `garrison-c` ceremony in ₣A- (pace ₢A-AAx) writes its own forced command, so it should adopt this same two-layer-wrapper + `:(RX)`-ACL pattern when it lands.

Want me to record this finding — the two-layer wrapper fix and the ACL/test-as-target lesson — into the ₣A- paddock or `Memos/memo-20260516-windows-headless-account-anatomy.md` so it's not rediscovered cold when that pace mounts?

file.content:
# Windows Headless SSH Account Anatomy

Date: 2026-05-16

## Why this memo exists

When standing up a Windows account that should be reachable via SSH
but should never have an interactive Windows logon (a "headless" or
"service-style" account), the path from `net user /add` to a working
`ssh user@host` is not direct. Each link in the chain has a separate
failure mode that surfaces as the same opaque "Permission denied
(publickey)" log line. This memo captures the chain so the next
investigator does not re-derive it from registry diving and Event
Log spelunking.

The pattern is load-bearing in `bujuw_user` infrastructure
(`Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh`) and surfaces again whenever an
operator wants a parallel test account for ad-hoc Windows SSH work.
The brad@rocket setup on 2026-05-16 was the most recent rediscovery.

## The chain, in causal order

### 1. `net.exe user /add` creates an account but no profile

```
net.exe user brad "" /add /passwordreq:no /active:yes
```

Creates a Windows account, assigns it a SID, and stops there. It
does NOT create:

- `C:\Users\<name>\` profile directory
- Any entry under `HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\ProfileList\{SID}`

The profile is registered only on first interactive logon (or by an
explicit `userenv.dll!CreateProfile` call). SSH key authentication
alone does NOT trigger profile creation.

### 2. Windows OpenSSH resolves relative AuthorizedKeysFile via the profile

The shipped sshd_config has `AuthorizedKeysFile .ssh/authorized_keys`
— a relative path. Windows OpenSSH resolves it by calling
`GetUserProfileDirectoryW(token)`, which queries
`HKLM:\…\ProfileList\{SID}\ProfileImagePath`.

For a headless account (no ProfileList entry), this returns failure.
sshd has no home path to use; auth fails. The Event Log
(`OpenSSH/Operational`) shows only `Failed publickey for {user}`
with no further explanation. The pubkey was offered correctly and
the file at `C:\Users\<name>\.ssh\authorized_keys` may even exist
with correct content and correct ACLs — sshd never looks there
because the home-directory resolution failed before any file lookup
was attempted.

### 3. Match block with absolute AuthorizedKeysFile bypasses the profile lookup

The structural fix is a sshd_config Match block giving an absolute
path:

```
