content.text:
Confirmed the memo is the right home — its own stated purpose is the chain "whenever an operator wants a parallel test account for ad-hoc Windows SSH work," which is exactly this. Three comments before I start, one of which is a real question:

**Comment 1 — the memo edit is partly a *correction*, not just an addition.** The memo currently documents the cygwin account with the **old, broken** inline forced-command (§"cygwin@rocket" / "Forced-command pattern", ~line 208-213): `command="C:\cygwin64\bin\bash --login -c \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\""`, presented as if it works. I just replaced that on the box. So I'll **correct that passage** (mark the inline form as the trap, document the two-layer `runcmd.sh` wrapper + the `rocket\<user>:(RX)` ACL + the "can't validate as admin, must test through the target account" lesson). Flagging because it means rewriting an existing section, not just appending — fully in keeping with the covenant that I tell you when I'm changing something that reads as currently-true.

**Comment 2 — framing toward your actual goal.** You want to *do manual testing today*, not plan node-config. So I'll add a consolidated **"Ad-hoc manual testing access — current state"** section near the top: the three accounts (`brad`/`cygwin`/`wsl`@rocket), what shell each gives now, the `tt/buw-jpS` admin lever, and the live Docker-in-WSL fact — a "how do I reach the box right now" reference, kept explicitly separate from the formal BURN/BURP garrison material so the two don't bleed. That's the artifact that serves you today.

**Question — "all windows named heats" is the one I'd push back on.** Only **₣A-** is genuinely about Windows; the other four carry `win` in the silks but aren't Windows-account work (₣BN depot-regime, ₣BC theurge self-test, ₣BG orchestration-doc scrub, ₣BI tadmor/scry). ₣A- *already* cites this memo in its Standing Notes (I'd fold the new finding in there). Dropping a "Windows headless account anatomy" ref into the depot-regime or doc-scrub paddock would be a non-load-bearing reference with no natural home — the kind of noise the load-bearing principle warns against. So:

1	# Windows Headless SSH Account Anatomy
2	
3	Date: 2026-05-16
4	
5	## Why this memo exists
6	
7	When standing up a Windows account that should be reachable via SSH
8	but should never have an interactive Windows logon (a "headless" or
9	"service-style" account), the path from `net user /add` to a working
10	`ssh user@host` is not direct. Each link in the chain has a separate
11	failure mode that surfaces as the same opaque "Permission denied
12	(publickey)" log line. This memo captures the chain so the next
13	investigator does not re-derive it from registry diving and Event
14	Log spelunking.
15	
16	The pattern is load-bearing in `bujuw_user` infrastructure
17	(`Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh`) and surfaces again whenever an
18	operator wants a parallel test account for ad-hoc Windows SSH work.
19	The brad@rocket setup on 2026-05-16 was the most recent rediscovery.
20	
21	## The chain, in causal order
22	
23	### 1. `net.exe user /add` creates an account but no profile
24	
25	```
26	net.exe user brad "" /add /passwordreq:no /active:yes
27	```
28	
29	Creates a Windows account, assigns it a SID, and stops there. It
30	does NOT create:
31	
32	- `C:\Users\<name>\` profile directory
33	- Any entry under `HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\ProfileList\{SID}`
34	
35	The profile is registered only on first interactive logon (or by an
36	explicit `userenv.dll!CreateProfile` call). SSH key authentication
37	alone does NOT trigger profile creation.
38	
39	### 2. Windows OpenSSH resolves relative AuthorizedKeysFile via the profile
40	
41	The shipped sshd_config has `AuthorizedKeysFile .ssh/authorized_keys`
42	— a relative path. Windows OpenSSH resolves it by calling
43	`GetUserProfileDirectoryW(token)`, which queries
44	`HKLM:\…\ProfileList\{SID}\ProfileImagePath`.
45	
46	For a headless account (no ProfileList entry), this returns failure.
47	sshd has no home path to use; auth fails. The Event Log
48	(`OpenSSH/Operational`) shows only `Failed publickey for {user}`
49	with no further explanation. The pubkey was offered correctly and
50	the file at `C:\Users\<name>\.ssh\authorized_keys` may even exist
51	with correct content and correct ACLs — sshd never looks there
52	because the home-directory resolution failed before any file lookup
53	was attempted.
54	
55	### 3. Match block with absolute AuthorizedKeysFile bypasses the profile lookup
56	
57	The structural fix is a sshd_config Match block giving an absolute
58	path:
59	
60	```
61	Match User <name>
62	    AuthorizedKeysFile __PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/<name>/authorized_keys
63	```
64	
65	`__PROGRAMDATA__` is sshd's token for `C:\ProgramData`. The
66	absolute path means sshd never asks the OS where the home is — it
67	opens the file directly. This is Microsoft's documented pattern for
68	service-style accounts.
69	
70	This is the design choice in `BUJB_sshd_match_block_text`
71	(`Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh:124-125`). It is not a Cygwin or
72	Windows path organization preference — it is structurally required
73	for any account that will never have an interactive profile. The
74	spec text presents the Match block as a routing detail; the
75	underlying reason is the `GetUserProfileDirectoryW` chain.
76	
77	### 4. Orphan `C:\Users\<name>` causes silent `.machinename` profile suffix
78	
79	If `C:\Users\<name>` exists as a directory at the moment a Windows
80	session first creates the account's profile, User Profile Service
81	disambiguates by minting `C:\Users\<name>.<machinename>` instead.
82	The original directory becomes an orphan.
83	
84	This bit during the brad@rocket setup: a pre-existing
85	`C:\Users\brad\` from an earlier failed
86	`%h/.ssh/authorized_keys` attempt caused brad's actual profile to
87	land at `C:\Users\brad.rocket\`.
88	
89	Operational consequence for `bujuw_user`: garrison's destructive
90	cleanup must remove both `C:\Users\<workload>` and any
91	`C:\Users\<workload>.*` siblings before the next profile creation.
92	A residual orphan silently splits the workload's home for the next
93	garrison cycle.
94	
95	### 5. Cygwin and Windows disagree on "home" for the same user
96	
97	After profile creation, two different APIs return two different
98	home paths:
99	
100	| Caller                  | Returns for brad on rocket           |
101	|-------------------------|--------------------------------------|
102	| `getpwnam("brad")`      | `/home/brad` (POSIX default)         |
103	| `$HOME` in shell        | `/cygdrive/c/Users/brad.rocket`      |
104	| `~brad` (tilde expand)  | `/home/brad`                         |
105	
106	SSH tools (ssh, ssh-keygen, scp, git-over-ssh) use `getpwnam` and
107	land on `/home/brad`. Login bash inherits `$HOME` from the Windows
108	session and lands on the Windows profile path. They disagree.
109	
110	Symptoms during the brad setup: `mkdir -p ~/.ssh` succeeded but
111	ssh-keygen with default path still failed because `~` and
112	ssh-keygen's path resolution diverged.
113	
114	Mitigation for a target account: set `HOME=/home/<name>` in
115	`.bash_profile` and place `.ssh/` there. SSH tools and bash then
116	agree on a single home.
117	
118	This concern applies to `bujuw_user` too. Worth a directed probe
119	during practice runs:
120	
121	```
122	ssh -t bujuw_user@rocket 'echo $HOME; getent passwd bujuw_user | cut -d: -f6'
123	```
124	
125	If split-HOME is observed, the workload's planted privkey at
126	`z_wlhome/.ssh/id_ed25519` may not be findable by `git push` (which
127	uses `$HOME` expansion). If login bash already auto-aligns via
128	`/etc/profile`, no action needed.
129	
130	### 6. StrictModes ACL allowlist
131	
132	sshd's strict-mode check on authkeys files allows ACL entries only
133	for: the user, SYSTEM, BUILTIN\Administrators. Any other principal
134	in the ACL causes silent refusal — even read-only entries like
135	`Everyone:(RX)` or `BUILTIN\Users:(RX)`.
136	
137	Inherited ACLs from `C:\Users\` and `C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\`
138	include these by default. Explicit lockdown is required:
139	
140	```
141	icacls C:\Path\To\authorized_keys /inheritance:r /grant:r SYSTEM:F "BUILTIN\Administrators":F <user>:R
142	```
143	
144	Owner must be the user OR a member of Administrators. Both are
145	accepted.
146	
147	## Worked example: stand up a parallel test account
148	
149	(brad@rocket, 2026-05-16. Substitute names as needed.)
150	
151	Admin SSH session on rocket:
152	
153	```
154	net.exe user brad "" /add /passwordreq:no /active:yes
155	mkdir C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\brad
156	# Write authorized_keys content: command="<shell-launch>" <pubkey>
157	icacls C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\brad\authorized_keys /inheritance:r /grant:r SYSTEM:F "BUILTIN\Administrators":F brad:R
158	icacls C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\brad /inheritance:r /grant:r SYSTEM:F "BUILTIN\Administrators":F
159	# Append Match block to C:\ProgramData\ssh\sshd_config:
160	#   Match User brad
161	#       AuthorizedKeysFile __PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/brad/authorized_keys
162	sshd.exe -t -f C:\ProgramData\ssh\sshd_config   # validate
163	Restart-Service sshd
164	```
165	
166	From any client whose private key matches the pubkey:
167	
168	```
169	ssh brad@rocket
170	```
171	
172	Inside the new session, align HOME to avoid the Cygwin split:
173	
174	```
175	mkdir -p /home/brad/.ssh
176	chmod 700 /home/brad/.ssh
177	printf 'export HOME=/home/brad\ncd "$HOME"\n' > /home/brad/.bash_profile
178	```
179	
180	## Noninteractive forced-command variants
181	
182	The brad@rocket worked example above puts the account on an interactive
183	Cygwin shell — useful for ad-hoc human work, less useful for programmatic
184	access where each SSH connection should run a single command and exit.
185	The same chain (account creation, `__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/<name>/`
186	authkeys, ACL lockdown, Match block) also supports noninteractive
187	substrate-routing personas where the forced-command field encodes which
188	substrate the session lands in. Two variants were stood up alongside
189	brad@rocket on 2026-05-17 and verified:
190	
191	- `cygwin@rocket` — `ssh cygwin@rocket "uname -o"` → `Cygwin`
192	- `wsl@rocket` — `ssh wsl@rocket "uname -o"` → `GNU/Linux`
193	
194	The differences from brad@rocket fall in three places.
195	
196	### Forced-command pattern
197	
198	Brad's forced-command launches an interactive shell:
199	
200	```
201	command="C:\cygwin64\bin\bash --login -i"
202	```
203	
204	The noninteractive variants intercept the SSH-protocol-level original
205	command (which sshd exposes as the `SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` environment
206	variable in the spawned process) and route it through the substrate.
207	
208	**cygwin@rocket.** The forced-command launches a non-interactive Cygwin
209	login shell and feeds it `$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` via `-c`:
210	
211	```
212	command="C:\cygwin64\bin\bash --login -c \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\""
213	```
214	
215	The `\"` is sshd's authkeys-string escape for `"`; after parsing, the
216	inner command is `bash --login -c "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"`. When sshd
217	spawns the forced-command process, the `SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` env var
218	is set on the child. Bash expands the variable inside the `-c` script
219	body and executes whatever the client supplied.
220	
221	**wsl@rocket.** Embedding the wsl.exe invocation directly in the
222	authkeys command= field works in principle but compounds escape layers
223	(the inner `$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` must survive sshd's authkeys parsing,
224	cmd.exe's interpretation, and wsl.exe's argument splitting). A wrapper
225	`.cmd` file at `C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\wsl\runcmd.cmd` isolates the
226	runtime concern:
227	
228	```
229	@echo off
230	set WSLENV=SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND/u
231	C:\Windows\System32\wsl.exe -d Ubuntu-24.04 -u root -- bash -lc "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"
232	```
233	
234	The authkeys forced-command then becomes:
235	
236	```
237	command="C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\wsl\runcmd.cmd"
238	```
239	
240	The wrapper does two load-bearing things:
241	
242	- `set WSLENV=SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND/u` tells WSL to forward
243	  `SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` into the Linux side as a Unix-style env var.
244	  By default WSL only shares a fixed subset of env vars across the
245	  Windows ↔ Linux boundary; arbitrary vars require an explicit `WSLENV`
246	  declaration.
247	- The `"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"` inside the inner `bash -lc` argument is
248	  literal text at the Windows-side cmd.exe layer (cmd.exe does not
249	  expand `$`-style variables) and gets expanded later by the WSL bash,
250	  which sees the value WSLENV forwarded.
251	
252	The wrapper file is ACL-locked to the same allowlist as authkeys
253	(`SYSTEM:F`, `BUILTIN\Administrators:F`, plus `wsl:RX`).
254	
255	### Per-user WSL state and provisioning without elevation
256	
257	WSL distros are registered per Windows user under
258	`HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Lxss`. A distro
259	installed by user A is not visible to user B. The headless `wsl`
260	Windows account therefore needs its own distro registration — but
261	`wsl --install`, the obvious provisioning command, requires admin
262	elevation (it enables the WSL Windows feature, which is system-wide)
263	and the wsl account is not (and should not be) an admin.
264	
265	`wsl --import` works around this. It registers an existing distro
266	tarball into the *current* user's `Lxss` registry without elevation —
267	because the WSL Windows feature is already enabled at the system level,
268	and `--import` only adds the per-user registration. The bootstrap
269	pattern:
270	
271	1. As an admin user that already has the distro installed:
272	   ```
273	   wsl --export Ubuntu-24.04 C:\ProgramData\bootstrap\ubuntu.tar
274	   ```
275	2. Grant the headless user read on the tarball and modify on the install
276	   location:
277	   ```
278	   icacls C:\ProgramData\bootstrap\ubuntu.tar /grant wsl:R
279	   icacls C:\WSL /grant 'wsl:(OI)(CI)M'
280	   ```
281	3. Temporarily set the headless user's authkeys forced-command to a
282	   one-shot import:
283	   ```
284	   command="C:\Windows\System32\wsl.exe --import Ubuntu-24.04 C:\WSL\Ubuntu-24.04 C:\ProgramData\bootstrap\ubuntu.tar"
285	   ```
286	4. Trigger from the SSH client — any command fires the forced-command
287	   and the connection blocks until import completes:
288	   ```
289	   ssh wsl@host trigger
290	   ```
291	5. Restore the runtime forced-command (the `runcmd.cmd`-based variant
292	   above), re-lock its ACL, and delete the tarball.
293	
294	The wsl account installs its own WSL state through its own SSH session,
295	because `--import` does not need the rights that `--install` does.
296	Principle-clean: the registration runs as the target user, no rights
297	modifications on the headless account, no cross-user impersonation.
298	
299	### Operational notes
300	
301	**ACL re-lock after `[IO.File]::WriteAllText`.** Writing a fresh file
302	via the .NET method resets the file's ACL to inherited from its parent
303	directory, undoing the `icacls /inheritance:r` lockdown established in
304	section 6 above. Re-apply the icacls lockdown after every rewrite of
305	authkeys (or the wrapper `.cmd`, or any file living under the locked
306	ProgramData subtree). `Set-Content` has the same behavior. The sequence
307	is: rewrite file → reapply ACL.
308	
309	**Transit content past cmd.exe / PowerShell.** Writing arbitrary file
310	content from a remote bash session over `ssh ... cmd.exe ... powershell
311	... wsl.exe` is a quoting graveyard — `&&`, `|`, `$VAR`, `\"`, and `'`
312	all behave differently at each layer. The reliable transit is to
313	base64-encode content on the curia side and decode in PowerShell:
314	
315	```
316	B64=$(printf '%s\n' "$CONTENT" | openssl enc -base64 -A)
317	PS_CMD="[IO.File]::WriteAllText('C:\\path\\file', [System.Text.Encoding]::UTF8.GetString([Convert]::FromBase64String('${B64}')))"
318	ENCODED=$(printf '%s' "$PS_CMD" | iconv -f UTF-8 -t UTF-16LE | openssl enc -base64 -A)
319	ssh user@host powershell -NoProfile -EncodedCommand "$ENCODED"
320	```
321	
322	The two base64 layers protect different segments: the inner one keeps
323	file content opaque to PowerShell's parser; the outer one
324	(`-EncodedCommand`) keeps the entire PowerShell command opaque to
325	cmd.exe's tokenizer. The matching code in this project's bash utilities
326	uses `openssl enc -base64 -A` consistently — see
327	`Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh` for prior art in the same shape.
328	
329	**`wsl.exe` emits UTF-16LE.** Output from `wsl -l -v`, `wsl --status`,
330	and `wsl --install` is UTF-16LE on stdout. Through cmd-mode SSH
331	transport the interleaved null bytes terminate C-string handling and
332	the output appears empty or garbled (single visible characters spaced
333	out). Force UTF-8 with `$env:WSL_UTF8=1` set before the wsl invocation,
334	or invoke via a PowerShell wrapper that handles UTF-16 natively.
335	
336	## See also
337	
338	- `Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh:124-125` — `BUJB_sshd_match_block_text`
339	- `Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh:166-168` — `BUJB_command_b/c/w` forced-command directives
340	- `Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BUSJCW-CaparisonWindows.adoc` — caparison spec
341	- `Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BUSJGC-GarrisonCygwin.adoc` — garrison-c spec
342	- `Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BUSJGW-GarrisonWsl.adoc` — garrison-w spec
343	- Microsoft OpenSSH for Windows StrictModes documentation

file.content:
# Windows Headless SSH Account Anatomy

Date: 2026-05-16

## Why this memo exists

When standing up a Windows account that should be reachable via SSH
but should never have an interactive Windows logon (a "headless" or
"service-style" account), the path from `net user /add` to a working
`ssh user@host` is not direct. Each link in the chain has a separate
failure mode that surfaces as the same opaque "Permission denied
(publickey)" log line. This memo captures the chain so the next
investigator does not re-derive it from registry diving and Event
Log spelunking.

The pattern is load-bearing in `bujuw_user` infrastructure
(`Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh`) and surfaces again whenever an
operator wants a parallel test account for ad-hoc Windows SSH work.
The brad@rocket setup on 2026-05-16 was the most recent rediscovery.

## The chain, in causal order

### 1. `net.exe user /add` creates an account but no profile

```
net.exe user brad "" /add /passwordreq:no /active:yes
```

Creates a Windows account, assigns it a SID, and stops there. It
does NOT create:

- `C:\Users\<name>\` profile directory
- Any entry under `HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\ProfileList\{SID}`

The profile is registered only on first interactive logon (or by an
explicit `userenv.dll!CreateProfile` call). SSH key authentication
alone does NOT trigger profile creation.

### 2. Windows OpenSSH resolves relative AuthorizedKeysFile via the profile

The shipped sshd_config has `AuthorizedKeysFile .ssh/authorized_keys`
— a relative path. Windows OpenSSH resolves it by calling
`GetUserProfileDirectoryW(token)`, which queries
`HKLM:\…\ProfileList\{SID}\ProfileImagePath`.

For a headless account (no ProfileList entry), this returns failure.
sshd has no home path to use; auth fails. The Event Log
(`OpenSSH/Operational`) shows only `Failed publickey for {user}`
with no further explanation. The pubkey was offered correctly and
the file at `C:\Users\<name>\.ssh\authorized_keys` may even exist
with correct content and correct ACLs — sshd never looks there
because the home-directory resolution failed before any file lookup
was attempted.

### 3. Match block with absolute AuthorizedKeysFile bypasses the profile lookup

The structural fix is a sshd_config Match block giving an absolute
path:

```
Match User <name>
    AuthorizedKeysFile __PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/<name>/authorized_keys
```

`__PROGRAMDATA__` is sshd's token for `C:\ProgramData`. The
absolute path means sshd never asks the OS where the home is — it
opens the file directly. This is Microsoft's documented pattern for
service-style accounts.

This is the design choice in `BUJB_sshd_match_block_text`
(`Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh:124-125`). It is not a Cygwin or
Windows path organization preference — it is structurally required
for any account that will never have an interactive profile. The
spec text presents the Match block as a routing detail; the
underlying reason is the `GetUserProfileDirectoryW` chain.

### 4. Orphan `C:\Users\<name>` causes silent `.machinename` profile suffix

If `C:\Users\<name>` exists as a directory at the moment a Windows
session first creates the account's profile, User Profile Service
disambiguates by minting `C:\Users\<name>.<machinename>` instead.
The original directory becomes an orphan.

This bit during the brad@rocket setup: a pre-existing
`C:\Users\brad\` from an earlier failed
`%h/.ssh/authorized_keys` attempt caused brad's actual profile to
land at `C:\Users\brad.rocket\`.

Operational consequence for `bujuw_user`: garrison's destructive
cleanup must remove both `C:\Users\<workload>` and any
`C:\Users\<workload>.*` siblings before the next profile creation.
A residual orphan silently splits the workload's home for the next
garrison cycle.

### 5. Cygwin and Windows disagree on "home" for the same user

After profile creation, two different APIs return two different
home paths:

| Caller                  | Returns for brad on rocket           |
|-------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| `getpwnam("brad")`      | `/home/brad` (POSIX default)         |
| `$HOME` in shell        | `/cygdrive/c/Users/brad.rocket`      |
| `~brad` (tilde expand)  | `/home/brad`                         |

SSH tools (ssh, ssh-keygen, scp, git-over-ssh) use `getpwnam` and
land on `/home/brad`. Login bash inherits `$HOME` from the Windows
session and lands on the Windows profile path. They disagree.

Symptoms during the brad setup: `mkdir -p ~/.ssh` succeeded but
ssh-keygen with default path still failed because `~` and
ssh-keygen's path resolution diverged.

Mitigation for a target account: set `HOME=/home/<name>` in
`.bash_profile` and place `.ssh/` there. SSH tools and bash then
agree on a single home.

This concern applies to `bujuw_user` too. Worth a directed probe
during practice runs:

```
ssh -t bujuw_user@rocket 'echo $HOME; getent passwd bujuw_user | cut -d: -f6'
```

If split-HOME is observed, the workload's planted privkey at
`z_wlhome/.ssh/id_ed25519` may not be findable by `git push` (which
uses `$HOME` expansion). If login bash already auto-aligns via
`/etc/profile`, no action needed.

### 6. StrictModes ACL allowlist

sshd's strict-mode check on authkeys files allows ACL entries only
for: the user, SYSTEM, BUILTIN\Administrators. Any other principal
in the ACL causes silent refusal — even read-only entries like
`Everyone:(RX)` or `BUILTIN\Users:(RX)`.

Inherited ACLs from `C:\Users\` and `C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\`
include these by default. Explicit lockdown is required:

```
icacls C:\Path\To\authorized_keys /inheritance:r /grant:r SYSTEM:F "BUILTIN\Administrators":F <user>:R
```

Owner must be the user OR a member of Administrators. Both are
accepted.

## Worked example: stand up a parallel test account

(brad@rocket, 2026-05-16. Substitute names as needed.)

Admin SSH session on rocket:

```
net.exe user brad "" /add /passwordreq:no /active:yes
mkdir C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\brad
# Write authorized_keys content: command="<shell-launch>" <pubkey>
icacls C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\brad\authorized_keys /inheritance:r /grant:r SYSTEM:F "BUILTIN\Administrators":F brad:R
icacls C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\brad /inheritance:r /grant:r SYSTEM:F "BUILTIN\Administrators":F
# Append Match block to C:\ProgramData\ssh\sshd_config:
#   Match User brad
#       AuthorizedKeysFile __PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/brad/authorized_keys
sshd.exe -t -f C:\ProgramData\ssh\sshd_config   # validate
Restart-Service sshd
```

From any client whose private key matches the pubkey:

```
ssh brad@rocket
```

Inside the new session, align HOME to avoid the Cygwin split:

```
mkdir -p /home/brad/.ssh
chmod 700 /home/brad/.ssh
printf 'export HOME=/home/brad\ncd "$HOME"\n' > /home/brad/.bash_profile
```

## Noninteractive forced-command variants

The brad@rocket worked example above puts the account on an interactive
Cygwin shell — useful for ad-hoc human work, less useful for programmatic
access where each SSH connection should run a single command and exit.
The same chain (account creation, `__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/<name>/`
authkeys, ACL lockdown, Match block) also supports noninteractive
substrate-routing personas where the forced-command field encodes which
substrate the session lands in. Two variants were stood up alongside
brad@rocket on 2026-05-17 and verified:

- `cygwin@rocket` — `ssh cygwin@rocket "uname -o"` → `Cygwin`
- `wsl@rocket` — `ssh wsl@rocket "uname -o"` → `GNU/Linux`

The differences from brad@rocket fall in three places.

### Forced-command pattern

Brad's forced-command launches an interactive shell:

```
command="C:\cygwin64\bin\bash --login -i"
```

The noninteractive variants intercept the SSH-protocol-level original
command (which sshd exposes as the `SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` environment
variable in the spawned process) and route it through the substrate.

**cygwin@rocket.** The forced-command launches a non-interactive Cygwin
login shell and feeds it `$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` via `-c`:

```
command="C:\cygwin64\bin\bash --login -c \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\""
```

The `\"` is sshd's authkeys-string escape for `"`; after parsing, the
inner command is `bash --login -c "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"`. When sshd
spawns the forced-command process, the `SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` env var
is set on the child. Bash expands the variable inside the `-c` script
body and executes whatever the client supplied.

**wsl@rocket.** Embedding the wsl.exe invocation directly in the
authkeys command= field works in principle but compounds escape layers
(the inner `$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` must survive sshd's authkeys parsing,
cmd.exe's interpretation, and wsl.exe's argument splitting). A wrapper
`.cmd` file at `C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\wsl\runcmd.cmd` isolates the
runtime concern:

```
@echo off
set WSLENV=SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND/u
C:\Windows\System32\wsl.exe -d Ubuntu-24.04 -u root -- bash -lc "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"
```

The authkeys forced-command then becomes:

```
command="C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\wsl\runcmd.cmd"
```

The wrapper does two load-bearing things:

- `set WSLENV=SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND/u` tells WSL to forward
  `SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` into the Linux side as a Unix-style env var.
  By default WSL only shares a fixed subset of env vars across the
  Windows ↔ Linux boundary; arbitrary vars require an explicit `WSLENV`
  declaration.
- The `"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"` inside the inner `bash -lc` argument is
  literal text at the Windows-side cmd.exe layer (cmd.exe does not
  expand `$`-style variables) and gets expanded later by the WSL bash,
  which sees the value WSLENV forwarded.

The wrapper file is ACL-locked to the same allowlist as authkeys
(`SYSTEM:F`, `BUILTIN\Administrators:F`, plus `wsl:RX`).

### Per-user WSL state and provisioning without elevation

WSL distros are registered per Windows user under
`HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Lxss`. A distro
installed by user A is not visible to user B. The headless `wsl`
Windows account therefore needs its own distro registration — but
`wsl --install`, the obvious provisioning command, requires admin
elevation (it enables the WSL Windows feature, which is system-wide)
and the wsl account is not (and should not be) an admin.

`wsl --import` works around this. It registers an existing distro
tarball into the *current* user's `Lxss` registry without elevation —
because the WSL Windows feature is already enabled at the system level,
and `--import` only adds the per-user registration. The bootstrap
pattern:

1. As an admin user that already has the distro installed:
   ```
   wsl --export Ubuntu-24.04 C:\ProgramData\bootstrap\ubuntu.tar
   ```
2. Grant the headless user read on the tarball and modify on the install
   location:
   ```
   icacls C:\ProgramData\bootstrap\ubuntu.tar /grant wsl:R
   icacls C:\WSL /grant 'wsl:(OI)(CI)M'
   ```
3. Temporarily set the headless user's authkeys forced-command to a
   one-shot import:
   ```
   command="C:\Windows\System32\wsl.exe --import Ubuntu-24.04 C:\WSL\Ubuntu-24.04 C:\ProgramData\bootstrap\ubuntu.tar"
   ```
4. Trigger from the SSH client — any command fires the forced-command
   and the connection blocks until import completes:
   ```
   ssh wsl@host trigger
   ```
5. Restore the runtime forced-command (the `runcmd.cmd`-based variant
   above), re-lock its ACL, and delete the tarball.

The wsl account installs its own WSL state through its own SSH session,
because `--import` does not need the rights that `--install` does.
Principle-clean: the registration runs as the target user, no rights
modifications on the headless account, no cross-user impersonation.

### Operational notes

**ACL re-lock after `[IO.File]::WriteAllText`.** Writing a fresh file
via the .NET method resets the file's ACL to inherited from its parent
directory, undoing the `icacls /inheritance:r` lockdown established in
section 6 above. Re-apply the icacls lockdown after every rewrite of
authkeys (or the wrapper `.cmd`, or any file living under the locked
ProgramData subtree). `Set-Content` has the same behavior. The sequence
is: rewrite file → reapply ACL.

**Transit content past cmd.exe / PowerShell.** Writing arbitrary file
content from a remote bash session over `ssh ... cmd.exe ... powershell
... wsl.exe` is a quoting graveyard — `&&`, `|`, `$VAR`, `\"`, and `'`
all behave differently at each layer. The reliable transit is to
base64-encode content on the curia side and decode in PowerShell:

```
B64=$(printf '%s\n' "$CONTENT" | openssl enc -base64 -A)
PS_CMD="[IO.File]::WriteAllText('C:\\path\\file', [System.Text.Encoding]::UTF8.GetString([Convert]::FromBase64String('${B64}')))"
ENCODED=$(printf '%s' "$PS_CMD" | iconv -f UTF-8 -t UTF-16LE | openssl enc -base64 -A)
ssh user@host powershell -NoProfile -EncodedCommand "$ENCODED"
```

The two base64 layers protect different segments: the inner one keeps
file content opaque to PowerShell's parser; the outer one
(`-EncodedCommand`) keeps the entire PowerShell command opaque to
cmd.exe's tokenizer. The matching code in this project's bash utilities
uses `openssl enc -base64 -A` consistently — see
`Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh` for prior art in the same shape.

**`wsl.exe` emits UTF-16LE.** Output from `wsl -l -v`, `wsl --status`,
and `wsl --install` is UTF-16LE on stdout. Through cmd-mode SSH
transport the interleaved null bytes terminate C-string handling and
the output appears empty or garbled (single visible characters spaced
out). Force UTF-8 with `$env:WSL_UTF8=1` set before the wsl invocation,
or invoke via a PowerShell wrapper that handles UTF-16 natively.

## See also

- `Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh:124-125` — `BUJB_sshd_match_block_text`
- `Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh:166-168` — `BUJB_command_b/c/w` forced-command directives
- `Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BUSJCW-CaparisonWindows.adoc` — caparison spec
- `Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BUSJGC-GarrisonCygwin.adoc` — garrison-c spec
- `Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BUSJGW-GarrisonWsl.adoc` — garrison-w spec
- Microsoft OpenSSH for Windows StrictModes documentation

content.text:
Read the whole thing. Here's the honest answer to your question, plus something I owe you about my own "discovery" today.

## Does the memo remember the admin-account setup? No — and that's the one real gap.

First, my interpretation: by "special admin account" I take you to mean the **privileged `bhyslop` account** that `tt/buw-jpS` uses — the Windows admin we leaned on all session to write the ACL-locked files. (If you meant something else, correct me.)

The memo documents the **three headless/unprivileged accounts** — `brad`, `cygwin`, `wsl` — end to end: the `net user /add` → no-profile → Match-block → ACL → forced-command chain, plus the WSL `--import` provisioning trick. That's a clean recipe for *those*.

It says **nothing** about the privileged `bhyslop` admin account: no mention of `administrators_authorized_keys`, how its key trust was placed, or its `icacls` lockdown. That account is established by the **formal caparison-windows Phase 1 ceremony** (the memo's "See also" points at `BUSJCW-CaparisonWindows.adoc`), which is ₣A- territory — exactly the "clean node configuration" planning you said you're *not* interested in today. So the admin-setup steps live in the spec/ceremony, not here, and we didn't perform them this session (we used a pre-existing admin), so I don't have a fresh "our steps" capture to drop in.

## The thing I owe you: the memo already knew the ACL lesson — I hit a documented trap

When I called the `cygwin:(RX)` ACL failure a "new finding," that was wrong. The memo **already documents it**, twice:
- §"Operational notes" (line 301): *"Writing a fresh file via [IO.File]::WriteAll… resets the file's ACL to inherited… Re-apply the icacls lockdown after every rewrite."*
- §wsl wrapper (line 253): *"The wrapper file is ACL-locked to the same allowlist as authkeys (SYSTEM:F, BUILTIN\Administrators:F, **plus wsl:RX**)."*

I created `runcmd.sh` and skipped the re-lock — and hit precisely the trap the memo warns about. The memo was right; I didn't follow it. So that's not new knowledge to add; if anything it's evidence the recipe was already sound.

## So what actually needs changing is narrow

The genuinely **stale/wrong** part is the cygwin forced-command itself (lines 208-219): the memo presents the inline `command="C:\cygwin64\bin\bash --login -c \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\""` as working, but it only word-splits — no pipes, `;`, quotes, or `$var`. The corrected recipe is the **`runcmd.sh` two-layer wrapper paralleling wsl** (which is what I deployed and verified). That's the one real edit, plus a short "current state: all three accounts healthy, cygwin now matches wsl" note. That fits your "best recipe + current state, skip the failed attempts" instruction cleanly — no lessons-learned appendix needed, since the ACL lesson is already in §Operational notes.

## One question before I edit

The admin-account gap: do you want me to **close it** — i.e., reconstruct and verify the current `bhyslop` admin trust on `rocket` (read its `administrators_authorized_keys`, ACLs, sshd Match/hardening state via `tt/buw-jpS`) and write a "Privileged admin account — current state" recipe into the memo so the consolidated manual-testing reference is actually complete? Or **leave it pointed at the caparison ceremony** (a one-line "the admin account is established by caparison-windows Phase 1; see BUSJCW") and keep today's edit to just the cygwin correction?

Leaning toward the lightweight pointer, since reconstructing the admin recipe edges into the node-config planning you said you're setting aside today — but it's your call, and there's a real argument that a manual-testing reference is incomplete if it can't tell you how the lever it depends on was built.

We deferred a lot of windows tooling and we will have to redevelop and reconfirm some things later... but I do hope to do some base testing before all that.  Did I answer your questions?

content.text:
Yes — you answered it clearly: **consolidate everything into the one memo, including the admin account, so there's a single best-memory file rather than a scattered cloud.** That's the right instinct given how this work gets picked up cold months later. And I'll hold your framing: best recipe + current observed state, base-testing-oriented, not a re-plan of the deferred tooling.

One honest caveat I'll build around: for the admin account I can document its **current, observed** configuration precisely (I can read it off the box right now), but the original "how it was first established" steps we did *not* perform this session — so I'll mark anything I'm reconstructing-by-inference distinct from what I directly verified, rather than inventing a clean origin story.

Let me gather the admin account's actual state now (read-only, via `tt/buw-jpS`), then I'll do the consolidated memo edit.

# This is the sshd server system-wide configuration file.  See
# sshd_config(5) for more information.

# The strategy used for options in the default sshd_config shipped with
# OpenSSH is to specify options with their default value where
# possible, but leave them commented.  Uncommented options override the
# default value.

#Port 22
#AddressFamily any
#ListenAddress 0.0.0.0
#ListenAddress ::

#HostKey __PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/ssh_host_rsa_key
#HostKey __PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/ssh_host_dsa_key
#HostKey __PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/ssh_host_ecdsa_key
#HostKey __PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/ssh_host_ed25519_key

# Ciphers and keying
#RekeyLimit default none

# Logging
#SyslogFacility AUTH
#LogLevel INFO

# Authentication:

#LoginGraceTime 2m
#PermitRootLogin prohibit-password
#StrictModes yes
#MaxAuthTries 6
#MaxSessions 10

PubkeyAuthentication yes

# The default is to check both .ssh/authorized_keys and .ssh/authorized_keys2
# but this is overridden so installations will only check .ssh/authorized_keys
AuthorizedKeysFile	.ssh/authorized_keys

#AuthorizedPrincipalsFile none

# For this to work you will also need host keys in %programData%/ssh/ssh_known_hosts
#HostbasedAuthentication no
# Change to yes if you don't trust ~/.ssh/known_hosts for
# HostbasedAuthentication
#IgnoreUserKnownHosts no
# Don't read the user's ~/.rhosts and ~/.shosts files
#IgnoreRhosts yes

# To disable tunneled clear text passwords, change to no here!
PasswordAuthentication no
PermitEmptyPasswords no

# GSSAPI options
#GSSAPIAuthentication no

#AllowAgentForwarding yes
#AllowTcpForwarding yes
#GatewayPorts no
#PermitTTY yes
#PrintMotd yes
#PrintLastLog yes
#TCPKeepAlive yes
#UseLogin no
#PermitUserEnvironment no
#ClientAliveInterval 0
#ClientAliveCountMax 3
#UseDNS no
#PidFile /var/run/sshd.pid
#MaxStartups 10:30:100
#PermitTunnel no
#ChrootDirectory none
#VersionAddendum none

# no default banner path
#Banner none

# override default of no subsystems
Subsystem	sftp	sftp-server.exe

# Example of overriding settings on a per-user basis
#Match User anoncvs
#	AllowTcpForwarding no
#	PermitTTY no
#	ForceCommand cvs server

LogLevel DEBUG3
Match Group administrators
       AuthorizedKeysFile __PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/administrators_authorized_keys




Match User bujuw_user
    AuthorizedKeysFile __PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/bujuw_user/authorized_keys

Match User brad

Match User cygwin
    AuthorizedKeysFile __PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/cygwin/authorized_keys

Match User wsl

content.text: Now the consolidated current-state section (the "do base testing now" reference), inserted before the causal-chain recipe:

input.old_string:
The brad@rocket setup on 2026-05-16 was the most recent rediscovery.

## The chain, in causal order
input.new_string:
The brad@rocket setup on 2026-05-16 was the most recent rediscovery.

## Current access state on rocket (bujn-winpc)

Verified 2026-05-31 via `tt/buw-jpS` (admin) and direct `ssh` (ad-hoc
accounts). This is the live picture for manual testing; the recipes that
produced it follow in the sections below. Tailnet host `rocket` =
`bujn-winpc`. All authentication is pubkey-only.

### Accounts present

| Account      | Reach                          | Lands in                                                                    | Role |
|--------------|--------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------|------|
| `bhyslop`    | `tt/buw-jpS bujn-winpc <cmd>`  | Windows default shell (cmd.exe); prepend `powershell -Command` / `bash -c`  | **Privileged admin** — the lever that writes ACL-locked files |
| `brad`       | `ssh brad@rocket`              | Interactive Cygwin `bash --login -i`                                        | Human ad-hoc; forced-command ignores a passed command |
| `cygwin`     | `ssh cygwin@rocket "<cmd>"`    | Non-interactive Cygwin bash, full shell semantics                          | Programmatic Cygwin path (wrapper-fixed 2026-05-31) |
| `wsl`        | `ssh wsl@rocket "<cmd>"`       | WSL Ubuntu 24.04 as **root**, `bash -lc`                                    | Programmatic WSL path; **Docker 29.1.3 daemon live** |
| `bujuw_user` | `tt/buw-jws bujn-winpc`        | Garrison-determined shell                                                   | Formal BURN/BURP workload account (garrison-managed) — not ad-hoc |

The first four (admin + brad/cygwin/wsl) are the ad-hoc manual-testing
surface. `bujuw_user` is the formal jurisdiction path and is owned by the
garrison ceremony, not by hand-edits.

### sshd posture (global, `C:\ProgramData\ssh\sshd_config`)

- `PubkeyAuthentication yes`, `PasswordAuthentication no`, `PermitEmptyPasswords no`
- `AuthorizedKeysFile .ssh/authorized_keys` (default; overridden per-account
  by the Match blocks below)
- `LogLevel DEBUG3` — verbose, a debugging carry-over; harmless, revert to
  default when convenient (not load-bearing for access)

Match-block map:

| Match                  | AuthorizedKeysFile |
|------------------------|--------------------|
| `Group administrators` | `__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/administrators_authorized_keys` |
| `User bujuw_user`      | `__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/bujuw_user/authorized_keys` |
| `User brad`            | `__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/brad/authorized_keys` |
| `User cygwin`          | `__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/cygwin/authorized_keys` |
| `User wsl`             | `__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/wsl/authorized_keys` |

### Privileged admin account (`bhyslop`)

Reached by `tt/buw-jpS` (`bujb_privileged_ssh`, pass-through) using
`~/.ssh/id_ed25519_winpc-admin`, key-only / BatchMode. Directly observed on
2026-05-31:

- Member of `BUILTIN\Administrators` and `rocket\docker-users`; High integrity.
- Trust is a **bare** key (no forced command) in
  `C:\ProgramData\ssh\administrators_authorized_keys`:
  `ssh-ed25519 AAAA…R9aZJ bhyslop@winpc-admin`
- ACL: `BUILTIN\Administrators:(F)`, `NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM:(F)` — the canonical
  `administrators_authorized_keys` lockdown. The principal is the admins
  *group*, not a per-user grant.
- Authenticates via the `Match Group administrators` block.
- Default remote shell is cmd.exe; since the SSH path is pass-through, prepend
  `powershell -Command …` (or `C:\cygwin64\bin\bash -lc …`) as the task needs.

**Origin note (observed, not replayed).** This account's *establishment* —
admin pubkey placement, ACL lockdown, sshd hardening — is the
caparison-windows Phase 1 ceremony's job (`BUSJCW-CaparisonWindows.adoc`). It
pre-existed this consolidation; the above is the verified current state, not a
re-derivation of the original setup steps. When the formal ceremony is rebuilt
later, that is where the authoritative admin-setup recipe belongs; this section
records what is true on the box today so manual testing has a complete picture.

## The chain, in causal order

content.text: Now the key correction — replacing the broken cygwin description with the verified working recipe:

input.old_string:
**cygwin@rocket.** The forced-command launches a non-interactive Cygwin
login shell and feeds it `$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` via `-c`:

```
command="C:\cygwin64\bin\bash --login -c \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\""
```

The `\"` is sshd's authkeys-string escape for `"`; after parsing, the
inner command is `bash --login -c "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"`. When sshd
spawns the forced-command process, the `SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` env var
is set on the child. Bash expands the variable inside the `-c` script
body and executes whatever the client supplied.
input.new_string:
**cygwin@rocket.** The intuitive *inline* form is a trap. It was deployed
through 2026-05-31 and silently mangled every non-trivial command:

```
command="C:\cygwin64\bin\bash --login -c \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\""   # BROKEN
```

Why it fails: Windows OpenSSH runs the forced command through the default
shell, **cmd.exe**, which does not expand `$`-style variables (it uses
`%VAR%`). So bash's `-c` argument arrives as the literal token
`$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND`. The single bash then expands it *during execution*,
and the result is only word-split — never re-parsed for shell metacharacters.
Net effect: the client command runs as one bare command with whitespace-split
argv. No pipes, no `;`, no quoting, no `$var`, no redirection.
`ssh cygwin@rocket 'uname -o; whoami'` fails with `uname: unknown option --
;` because `;` is handed to uname as an argument. (Contrast wsl below, which
re-parses correctly — same symptom, opposite outcome, which is what made this
confusing.)

The fix mirrors the wsl variant: a wrapper that adds a second bash layer, so
the inner `bash -c` receives a fully-expanded, re-parseable script. Wrapper at
`C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\cygwin\runcmd.sh` (LF line endings — CRLF breaks the
shebang/exec):

```
#!/bin/bash
exec /bin/bash --login -c "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"
```

The wrapper's bash is a real shell: it expands `"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"`
(Cygwin inherits the Windows env var sshd set on the child) and hands the
*value* to the inner login `bash -c`, which parses it normally. The authkeys
forced-command points cmd.exe at the wrapper via Cygwin bash, passing the
script as a POSIX path:

```
command="C:\cygwin64\bin\bash /cygdrive/c/ProgramData/ssh/users/cygwin/runcmd.sh"
```

ACL-lock the wrapper to the StrictModes allowlist **plus the account's own
read+execute** — `SYSTEM:F`, `BUILTIN\Administrators:F`, `rocket\cygwin:(RX)`
— exactly as the wsl wrapper carries `wsl:RX`. This grant is load-bearing and
easy to miss: the forced command runs as the unprivileged `cygwin` user, so
testing the wrapper *as admin* passes while the real SSH connection fails
`Permission denied` on read. **Validate through the target account, never as
admin.** (Mechanism reproduced and fix verified working 2026-05-31:
`ssh cygwin@rocket 'echo hi | tr a-z A-Z'` → `HI`, matching wsl.)

content.text: Now refine the operational note to cover `WriteAllBytes` and the new-file-vs-overwrite ACL nuance I hit today:

input.old_string:
**ACL re-lock after `[IO.File]::WriteAllText`.** Writing a fresh file
via the .NET method resets the file's ACL to inherited from its parent
directory, undoing the `icacls /inheritance:r` lockdown established in
section 6 above. Re-apply the icacls lockdown after every rewrite of
authkeys (or the wrapper `.cmd`, or any file living under the locked
ProgramData subtree). `Set-Content` has the same behavior. The sequence
is: rewrite file → reapply ACL.
input.new_string:
**ACL re-lock after .NET / PowerShell file writes.** `[IO.File]::WriteAllText`,
`[IO.File]::WriteAllBytes`, and `Set-Content` all behave the same way, and the
behavior differs by whether the target already exists:

- **New file** (e.g. a freshly created `runcmd.sh`): inherits its parent
  directory's ACL, which does *not* include the account's own read+execute.
  You must apply the full lockdown including `<user>:(RX)` — otherwise the
  forced command runs as the unprivileged account and fails `Permission
  denied` reading its own wrapper. (This is exactly the 2026-05-31 cygwin
  wrapper miss — see "Noninteractive forced-command variants" above.)
- **Existing file overwritten in place** (e.g. truncating and rewriting an
  already-locked `authorized_keys`): the existing ACL/owner is preserved, so
  no re-lock is needed.

When in doubt, the safe sequence is always: write file → (re)apply the icacls
lockdown → verify with `icacls <file>`. And remember the trap above: a wrapper
that the unprivileged account must *read* needs that account in the ACL, which
you cannot confirm by testing as admin.

file.content:
(`Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh`) and surfaces again whenever an
operator wants a parallel test account for ad-hoc Windows SSH work.
The brad@rocket setup on 2026-05-16 was the most recent rediscovery.

## Current access state on rocket (bujn-winpc)

Verified 2026-05-31 via `tt/buw-jpS` (admin) and direct `ssh` (ad-hoc
accounts). This is the live picture for manual testing; the recipes that
produced it follow in the sections below. Tailnet host `rocket` =
`bujn-winpc`. All authentication is pubkey-only.

### Accounts present

| Account      | Reach                          | Lands in                                                                    | Role |
|--------------|--------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------|------|
| `bhyslop`    | `tt/buw-jpS bujn-winpc <cmd>`  | Windows default shell (cmd.exe); prepend `powershell -Command` / `bash -c`  | **Privileged admin** — the lever that writes ACL-locked files |
| `brad`       | `ssh brad@rocket`              | Interactive Cygwin `bash --login -i`                                        | Human ad-hoc; forced-command ignores a passed command |
| `cygwin`     | `ssh cygwin@rocket "<cmd>"`    | Non-interactive Cygwin bash, full shell semantics                          | Programmatic Cygwin path (wrapper-fixed 2026-05-31) |
| `wsl`        | `ssh wsl@rocket "<cmd>"`       | WSL Ubuntu 24.04 as **root**, `bash -lc`                                    | Programmatic WSL path; **Docker 29.1.3 daemon live** |
| `bujuw_user` | `tt/buw-jws bujn-winpc`        | Garrison-determined shell                                                   | Formal BURN/BURP workload account (garrison-managed) — not ad-hoc |

The first four (admin + brad/cygwin/wsl) are the ad-hoc manual-testing
surface. `bujuw_user` is the formal jurisdiction path and is owned by the
garrison ceremony, not by hand-edits.

### sshd posture (global, `C:\ProgramData\ssh\sshd_config`)

- `PubkeyAuthentication yes`, `PasswordAuthentication no`, `PermitEmptyPasswords no`
- `AuthorizedKeysFile .ssh/authorized_keys` (default; overridden per-account
  by the Match blocks below)
- `LogLevel DEBUG3` — verbose, a debugging carry-over; harmless, revert to
  default when convenient (not load-bearing for access)

Match-block map:

| Match                  | AuthorizedKeysFile |
|------------------------|--------------------|
| `Group administrators` | `__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/administrators_authorized_keys` |
| `User bujuw_user`      | `__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/bujuw_user/authorized_keys` |
| `User brad`            | `__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/brad/authorized_keys` |
| `User cygwin`          | `__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/cygwin/authorized_keys` |
| `User wsl`             | `__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/wsl/authorized_keys` |

### Privileged admin account (`bhyslop`)

Reached by `tt/buw-jpS` (`bujb_privileged_ssh`, pass-through) using
`~/.ssh/id_ed25519_winpc-admin`, key-only / BatchMode. Directly observed on
2026-05-31:

- Member of `BUILTIN\Administrators` and `rocket\docker-users`; High integrity.
- Trust is a **bare** key (no forced command) in
  `C:\ProgramData\ssh\administrators_authorized_keys`:
  `ssh-ed25519 AAAA…R9aZJ bhyslop@winpc-admin`
- ACL: `BUILTIN\Administrators:(F)`, `NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM:(F)` — the canonical
  `administrators_authorized_keys` lockdown. The principal is the admins
  *group*, not a per-user grant.
- Authenticates via the `Match Group administrators` block.
- Default remote shell is cmd.exe; since the SSH path is pass-through, prepend
  `powershell -Command …` (or `C:\cygwin64\bin\bash -lc …`) as the task needs.

**Origin note (observed, not replayed).** This account's *establishment* —
admin pubkey placement, ACL lockdown, sshd hardening — is the
caparison-windows Phase 1 ceremony's job (`BUSJCW-CaparisonWindows.adoc`). It
pre-existed this consolidation; the above is the verified current state, not a
re-derivation of the original setup steps. When the formal ceremony is rebuilt
later, that is where the authoritative admin-setup recipe belongs; this section
records what is true on the box today so manual testing has a complete picture.

## The chain, in causal order

### 1. `net.exe user /add` creates an account but no profile

```
net.exe user brad "" /add /passwordreq:no /active:yes
```

Creates a Windows account, assigns it a SID, and stops there. It
does NOT create:

- `C:\Users\<name>\` profile directory
- Any entry under `HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\ProfileList\{SID}`

The profile is registered only on first interactive logon (or by an
explicit `userenv.dll!CreateProfile` call). SSH key authentication
alone does NOT trigger profile creation.

### 2. Windows OpenSSH resolves relative AuthorizedKeysFile via the profile

The shipped sshd_config has `AuthorizedKeysFile .ssh/authorized_keys`
— a relative path. Windows OpenSSH resolves it by calling
`GetUserProfileDirectoryW(token)`, which queries
`HKLM:\…\ProfileList\{SID}\ProfileImagePath`.

For a headless account (no ProfileList entry), this returns failure.
sshd has no home path to use; auth fails. The Event Log

message.content: Do you think our ssh wsl@ and cygwin@ are interactive capable?

content.text: Good question — and I don't think they are, but let me verify rather than just reason. The key: both `wsl` and `cygwin` use a forced command of the shape `bash -c "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"`. With no command supplied (an interactive attempt), that's `bash -c ""` — runs nothing and exits, and won't read interactive stdin. Let me prove it, with `brad` (forced `bash --login -i`) as the interactive control.

input.command:
# Pipe a command into a no-arg session. An interactive shell reads stdin and runs it;
# a `bash -c ""` forced command ignores stdin and just exits. Pipe gives EOF, so no hang.
echo "=== wsl@rocket (no command, feed a line on stdin) ==="
printf 'echo STDIN_REACHED_A_SHELL\n' | ssh -o ConnectTimeout=8 -o BatchMode=yes wsl@rocket 2>&1; echo "exit:$?"
echo
echo "=== cygwin@rocket (no command, feed a line on stdin) ==="
printf 'echo STDIN_REACHED_A_SHELL\n' | ssh -o ConnectTimeout=8 -o BatchMode=yes cygwin@rocket 2>&1; echo "exit:$?"
echo
echo "=== brad@rocket (interactive control) ==="
printf 'echo STDIN_REACHED_A_SHELL\n' | ssh -o ConnectTimeout=8 -o BatchMode=yes brad@rocket 2>&1 | grep -a STDIN_REACHED; echo "exit:${PIPESTATUS[0]}"
input.description: Test interactive/stdin capability of each account

content.content:
=== wsl@rocket (no command, feed a line on stdin) ===
Pseudo-terminal will not be allocated because stdin is not a terminal.
exit:0

=== cygwin@rocket (no command, feed a line on stdin) ===
Pseudo-terminal will not be allocated because stdin is not a terminal.
exit:0

=== brad@rocket (interactive control) ===
STDIN_REACHED_A_SHELL
$ echo STDIN_REACHED_A_SHELL
exit:

content.text:
Verified — and the answer is **no**, neither is interactive-capable as configured. Only `brad@rocket` is.

The proof is in the output: piping a command into a no-arg session, `brad` ran it (`STDIN_REACHED_A_SHELL` printed) but `wsl` and `cygwin` produced **nothing** and exited 0. (The "Pseudo-terminal will not be allocated" line is the ssh *client* noting that a no-command session defaults to wanting a TTY — which actually reinforces the point: the client tried to go interactive, but the server's forced command refused.)

**Why:** `cygwin` and `wsl` both run a forced command of the form `bash -c "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"`. With no command supplied, that's `bash -c ""` → executes the empty string and exits immediately, never dropping to a prompt and never reading interactive stdin. `brad`'s forced command is `bash --login -i`, which *is* an interactive shell, so it honors a session. This is by design per the memo — `cygwin`/`wsl` are the "one command per connection" programmatic accounts; `brad` is the interactive one.

So the current interactive picture:

| Account | One-shot `ssh x@rocket "cmd"` | Interactive `ssh -t x@rocket` |
|---------|------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| `brad`  | ✗ (ignores the command) | ✓ Cygwin shell |
| `cygwin`| ✓ | ✗ (exits immediately) |
| `wsl`   | ✓ | ✗ (exits immediately) |

The gap worth noting for your manual testing: **you have no interactive WSL shell at all** — and that's the substrate with live Docker, where poking around interactively (run a container, exec into it, inspect) is most useful.

**I can close that gap** with a small enhancement: make each wrapper *conditional* — if `$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` is empty, exec an interactive login shell; otherwise run `-c`. That gives each account **both** modes (one-shot *and* `ssh -t` interactive) instead of forcing a choice. For cygwin it's trivial:

```bash
#!/bin/bash
if [ -z "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND" ]; then exec /bin/bash --login -i
else exec /bin/bash --login -c "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"; fi
```

The `wsl` side is the same idea but the cmd.exe→wsl.exe→bash escaping for the conditional needs care, so I'd build and verify it live rather than assume.

Want me to add interactive capability — both accounts, or just an interactive WSL shell (the missing-and-useful one)? I'd use the same admin lever + base64/ACL workflow, test through the target account, and fold the conditional-wrapper pattern into the memo. Or if you'd rather keep `cygwin`/`wsl` strictly programmatic and just use `brad` for interactive Cygwin, that's a clean answer too — there's just no interactive WSL in that world.

lastPrompt: Do you think our ssh wsl@ and cygwin@ are interactive capable?

message.content: Would you attempt this broadening of wsl@ and cygwin@ ? Test both flavors for both after; if it works, update memo, if not let me know and we'll decide then.

content.text:
On it. Plan: make each wrapper conditional (empty `$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` → interactive login shell; else `-c`). For cygwin that's a one-line change to `runcmd.sh`. For wsl I'll avoid the cmd.exe→wsl.exe→bash nested-quote graveyard (the exact thing the memo warns about) by putting the conditional in a clean **Linux-side** script inside the distro and having `runcmd.cmd` just call it. Overwriting existing files in place preserves their ACLs, so no re-grant needed — but I'll verify.

Let me generate all payloads first.

input.command:
python3 - <<'PYEOF'
import base64
def enc(ps): return base64.b64encode(ps.encode('utf-16-le')).decode()
def b64(s): return base64.b64encode(s.encode()).decode()

# --- cygwin conditional runcmd.sh (LF) ---
cyg = ('#!/bin/bash\n'
       'if [ -z "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND" ]; then\n'
       '  exec /bin/bash --login -i\n'
       'else\n'
       '  exec /bin/bash --login -c "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"\n'
       'fi\n')
CYG_PATH = r'C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\cygwin\runcmd.sh'
ps_cyg = "[IO.File]::WriteAllBytes('%s',[Convert]::FromBase64String('%s'))" % (CYG_PATH, b64(cyg))

# --- WSL linux-side sshrun (LF) — written via wsl@rocket base64 -d ---
sshrun = ('#!/bin/bash\n'
          'if [ -z "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND" ]; then\n'
          '  exec bash -li\n'
          'else\n'
          '  exec bash -lc "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"\n'
          'fi\n')
SSHRUN_B64 = b64(sshrun)

# --- new runcmd.cmd (CRLF, Windows batch) calling the linux script ---
cmd = ('@echo off\r\n'
       'set WSLENV=SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND/u\r\n'
       'C:\\Windows\\System32\\wsl.exe -d Ubuntu-24.04 -u root -- /usr/local/bin/sshrun\r\n')
CMD_PATH = r'C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\wsl\runcmd.cmd'
ps_cmd = "[IO.File]::WriteAllBytes('%s',[Convert]::FromBase64String('%s'))" % (CMD_PATH, b64(cmd))

# rollback: original simple runcmd.cmd (CRLF)
cmd_orig = ('@echo off\r\n'
            'set WSLENV=SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND/u\r\n'
            'C:\\Windows\\System32\\wsl.exe -d Ubuntu-24.04 -u root -- bash -lc "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"\r\n')
ps_cmd_orig = "[IO.File]::WriteAllBytes('%s',[Convert]::FromBase64String('%s'))" % (CMD_PATH, b64(cmd_orig))

out = {
 'enc_cyg.txt': enc(ps_cyg),
 'sshrun_b64.txt': SSHRUN_B64,
 'enc_cmd.txt': enc(ps_cmd),
 'enc_cmd_rollback.txt': enc(ps_cmd_orig),
}
for fn,v in out.items():
    open('/tmp/'+fn,'w').write(v)

print("cygwin runcmd.sh:"); print(cyg)
print("wsl /usr/local/bin/sshrun:"); print(sshrun)
print("wsl runcmd.cmd:"); print(repr(cmd))
print("payloads:", ", ".join('/tmp/'+k for k in out))
PYEOF

The `-tt` interactive test is inconclusive headlessly — the escape sequences show a conhost/conpty session *did* start, but forcing a fake pty over a pipe doesn't deliver our input as keystrokes. Both flavors pass for both accounts:

| Account | One-shot | Interactive (live shell consumes input) |
|---------|----------|------------------------------------------|
| `cygwin` | ✓ `ONESHOT_OK / HI / Cygwin` | ✓ `$` prompt + ran our input |
| `wsl` | ✓ `ONESHOT_OK / root / Docker 29.1.3 / HI` | ✓ ran our input as root |

**Honest caveat on the verification method:** I confirmed interactive capability via the headless proxy (a live `bash -i`/`-li` consuming piped commands on a no-command session) — the same way `brad` was shown interactive earlier. I did *not* fully exercise a real human-TTY `ssh -t` session (forcing a fake pty over a pipe with `-tt` is inconclusive — it just spawns conhost and closes). For `cygwin`, `brad` already proves real-TTY Cygwin interactive works on this box and `cygwin` now uses the identical `bash --login -i`, so I'm confident. For `wsl`, the conpty→wsl.exe→`bash -li` path is the standard interactive WSL route and the proxy passed, but a 10-second `ssh -t wsl@rocket` from your real terminal would be the final proof of the human experience.

content.content:
248	## Noninteractive forced-command variants
249	
250	The brad@rocket worked example above puts the account on an interactive
251	Cygwin shell — useful for ad-hoc human work, less useful for programmatic
252	access where each SSH connection should run a single command and exit.
253	The same chain (account creation, `__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/<name>/`
254	authkeys, ACL lockdown, Match block) also supports noninteractive
255	substrate-routing personas where the forced-command field encodes which
256	substrate the session lands in. Two variants were stood up alongside
257	brad@rocket on 2026-05-17 and verified:
258	
259	- `cygwin@rocket` — `ssh cygwin@rocket "uname -o"` → `Cygwin`
260	- `wsl@rocket` — `ssh wsl@rocket "uname -o"` → `GNU/Linux`
261	
262	The differences from brad@rocket fall in three places.
263	
264	### Forced-command pattern
265	
266	Brad's forced-command launches an interactive shell:
267	
268	```
269	command="C:\cygwin64\bin\bash --login -i"
270	```
271	
272	The noninteractive variants intercept the SSH-protocol-level original
273	command (which sshd exposes as the `SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` environment
274	variable in the spawned process) and route it through the substrate.
275	
276	**cygwin@rocket.** The intuitive *inline* form is a trap. It was deployed
277	through 2026-05-31 and silently mangled every non-trivial command:
278	
279	```
280	command="C:\cygwin64\bin\bash --login -c \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\""   # BROKEN
281	```
282	
283	Why it fails: Windows OpenSSH runs the forced command through the default
284	shell, **cmd.exe**, which does not expand `$`-style variables (it uses
285	`%VAR%`). So bash's `-c` argument arrives as the literal token
286	`$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND`. The single bash then expands it *during execution*,
287	and the result is only word-split — never re-parsed for shell metacharacters.
288	Net effect: the client command runs as one bare command with whitespace-split
289	argv. No pipes, no `;`, no quoting, no `$var`, no redirection.
290	`ssh cygwin@rocket 'uname -o; whoami'` fails with `uname: unknown option --
291	;` because `;` is handed to uname as an argument. (Contrast wsl below, which
292	re-parses correctly — same symptom, opposite outcome, which is what made this
293	confusing.)
294	
295	The fix mirrors the wsl variant: a wrapper that adds a second bash layer, so
296	the inner `bash -c` receives a fully-expanded, re-parseable script. Wrapper at
297	`C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\cygwin\runcmd.sh` (LF line endings — CRLF breaks the
298	shebang/exec):
299	
300	```
301	#!/bin/bash
302	exec /bin/bash --login -c "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"
303	```
304	
305	The wrapper's bash is a real shell: it expands `"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"`
306	(Cygwin inherits the Windows env var sshd set on the child) and hands the
307	*value* to the inner login `bash -c`, which parses it normally. The authkeys
308	forced-command points cmd.exe at the wrapper via Cygwin bash, passing the
309	script as a POSIX path:
310	
311	```
312	command="C:\cygwin64\bin\bash /cygdrive/c/ProgramData/ssh/users/cygwin/runcmd.sh"
313	```
314	
315	ACL-lock the wrapper to the StrictModes allowlist **plus the account's own
316	read+execute** — `SYSTEM:F`, `BUILTIN\Administrators:F`, `rocket\cygwin:(RX)`
317	— exactly as the wsl wrapper carries `wsl:RX`. This grant is load-bearing and
318	easy to miss: the forced command runs as the unprivileged `cygwin` user, so
319	testing the wrapper *as admin* passes while the real SSH connection fails
320	`Permission denied` on read. **Validate through the target account, never as
321	admin.** (Mechanism reproduced and fix verified working 2026-05-31:
322	`ssh cygwin@rocket 'echo hi | tr a-z A-Z'` → `HI`, matching wsl.)
323	
324	**wsl@rocket.** Embedding the wsl.exe invocation directly in the
325	authkeys command= field works in principle but compounds escape layers
326	(the inner `$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` must survive sshd's authkeys parsing,
327	cmd.exe's interpretation, and wsl.exe's argument splitting). A wrapper
328	`.cmd` file at `C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\wsl\runcmd.cmd` isolates the
329	runtime concern:
330	
331	```
332	@echo off
333	set WSLENV=SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND/u
334	C:\Windows\System32\wsl.exe -d Ubuntu-24.04 -u root -- bash -lc "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"
335	```
336	
337	The authkeys forced-command then becomes:
338	
339	```
340	command="C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\wsl\runcmd.cmd"
341	```
342	
343	The wrapper does two load-bearing things:
344	
345	- `set WSLENV=SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND/u` tells WSL to forward
346	  `SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` into the Linux side as a Unix-style env var.
347	  By default WSL only shares a fixed subset of env vars across the
348	  Windows ↔ Linux boundary; arbitrary vars require an explicit `WSLENV`
349	  declaration.
350	- The `"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"` inside the inner `bash -lc` argument is
351	  literal text at the Windows-side cmd.exe layer (cmd.exe does not
352	  expand `$`-style variables) and gets expanded later by the WSL bash,
353	  which sees the value WSLENV forwarded.
354	
355	The wrapper file is ACL-locked to the same allowlist as authkeys
356	(`SYSTEM:F`, `BUILTIN\Administrators:F`, plus `wsl:RX`).
357

file.content:
## Noninteractive forced-command variants

The brad@rocket worked example above puts the account on an interactive
Cygwin shell — useful for ad-hoc human work, less useful for programmatic
access where each SSH connection should run a single command and exit.
The same chain (account creation, `__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/<name>/`
authkeys, ACL lockdown, Match block) also supports noninteractive
substrate-routing personas where the forced-command field encodes which
substrate the session lands in. Two variants were stood up alongside
brad@rocket on 2026-05-17 and verified:

- `cygwin@rocket` — `ssh cygwin@rocket "uname -o"` → `Cygwin`
- `wsl@rocket` — `ssh wsl@rocket "uname -o"` → `GNU/Linux`

The differences from brad@rocket fall in three places.

### Forced-command pattern

Brad's forced-command launches an interactive shell:

```
command="C:\cygwin64\bin\bash --login -i"
```

The noninteractive variants intercept the SSH-protocol-level original
command (which sshd exposes as the `SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` environment
variable in the spawned process) and route it through the substrate.

**cygwin@rocket.** The intuitive *inline* form is a trap. It was deployed
through 2026-05-31 and silently mangled every non-trivial command:

```
command="C:\cygwin64\bin\bash --login -c \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\""   # BROKEN
```

Why it fails: Windows OpenSSH runs the forced command through the default
shell, **cmd.exe**, which does not expand `$`-style variables (it uses
`%VAR%`). So bash's `-c` argument arrives as the literal token
`$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND`. The single bash then expands it *during execution*,
and the result is only word-split — never re-parsed for shell metacharacters.
Net effect: the client command runs as one bare command with whitespace-split
argv. No pipes, no `;`, no quoting, no `$var`, no redirection.
`ssh cygwin@rocket 'uname -o; whoami'` fails with `uname: unknown option --
;` because `;` is handed to uname as an argument. (Contrast wsl below, which
re-parses correctly — same symptom, opposite outcome, which is what made this
confusing.)

The fix mirrors the wsl variant: a wrapper that adds a second bash layer, so
the inner `bash -c` receives a fully-expanded, re-parseable script. Wrapper at
`C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\cygwin\runcmd.sh` (LF line endings — CRLF breaks the
shebang/exec):

```
#!/bin/bash
exec /bin/bash --login -c "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"
```

The wrapper's bash is a real shell: it expands `"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"`
(Cygwin inherits the Windows env var sshd set on the child) and hands the
*value* to the inner login `bash -c`, which parses it normally. The authkeys
forced-command points cmd.exe at the wrapper via Cygwin bash, passing the
script as a POSIX path:

```
command="C:\cygwin64\bin\bash /cygdrive/c/ProgramData/ssh/users/cygwin/runcmd.sh"
```

ACL-lock the wrapper to the StrictModes allowlist **plus the account's own
read+execute** — `SYSTEM:F`, `BUILTIN\Administrators:F`, `rocket\cygwin:(RX)`
— exactly as the wsl wrapper carries `wsl:RX`. This grant is load-bearing and
easy to miss: the forced command runs as the unprivileged `cygwin` user, so
testing the wrapper *as admin* passes while the real SSH connection fails
`Permission denied` on read. **Validate through the target account, never as
admin.** (Mechanism reproduced and fix verified working 2026-05-31:
`ssh cygwin@rocket 'echo hi | tr a-z A-Z'` → `HI`, matching wsl.)

**wsl@rocket.** Embedding the wsl.exe invocation directly in the
authkeys command= field works in principle but compounds escape layers
(the inner `$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` must survive sshd's authkeys parsing,
cmd.exe's interpretation, and wsl.exe's argument splitting). A wrapper
`.cmd` file at `C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\wsl\runcmd.cmd` isolates the
runtime concern:

```
@echo off
set WSLENV=SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND/u
C:\Windows\System32\wsl.exe -d Ubuntu-24.04 -u root -- bash -lc "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"
```

The authkeys forced-command then becomes:

```
command="C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\wsl\runcmd.cmd"
```

The wrapper does two load-bearing things:

- `set WSLENV=SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND/u` tells WSL to forward
  `SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` into the Linux side as a Unix-style env var.
  By default WSL only shares a fixed subset of env vars across the
  Windows ↔ Linux boundary; arbitrary vars require an explicit `WSLENV`
  declaration.
- The `"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"` inside the inner `bash -lc` argument is
  literal text at the Windows-side cmd.exe layer (cmd.exe does not
  expand `$`-style variables) and gets expanded later by the WSL bash,
  which sees the value WSLENV forwarded.

The wrapper file is ACL-locked to the same allowlist as authkeys
(`SYSTEM:F`, `BUILTIN\Administrators:F`, plus `wsl:RX`).

content.text: Both flavors verified for both accounts. Updating the memo now — four edits: the section title/intro (no longer "noninteractive"), the cygwin conditional wrapper, the wsl Linux-side `sshrun` approach, and the accounts table.

input.old_string:
## Noninteractive forced-command variants

The brad@rocket worked example above puts the account on an interactive
Cygwin shell — useful for ad-hoc human work, less useful for programmatic
access where each SSH connection should run a single command and exit.
The same chain (account creation, `__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/<name>/`
authkeys, ACL lockdown, Match block) also supports noninteractive
substrate-routing personas where the forced-command field encodes which
substrate the session lands in. Two variants were stood up alongside
brad@rocket on 2026-05-17 and verified:

- `cygwin@rocket` — `ssh cygwin@rocket "uname -o"` → `Cygwin`
- `wsl@rocket` — `ssh wsl@rocket "uname -o"` → `GNU/Linux`

The differences from brad@rocket fall in three places.
input.new_string:
## Substrate-routing forced-command variants (cygwin / wsl)

The brad@rocket worked example puts the account on a hardcoded interactive
Cygwin shell. The same chain (account creation,
`__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/users/<name>/` authkeys, ACL lockdown, Match block) also
supports substrate-routing personas whose forced-command wrapper encodes which
substrate the session lands in. Two were stood up on 2026-05-17 and, after the
2026-05-31 repair + broadening, both are **dual-mode** — one-shot *and*
interactive on the same account:

- `cygwin@rocket` — `ssh cygwin@rocket "<cmd>"` runs a command in Cygwin;
  `ssh -t cygwin@rocket` drops to an interactive Cygwin login shell.
- `wsl@rocket` — `ssh wsl@rocket "<cmd>"` runs a command in WSL Ubuntu 24.04
  (as root); `ssh -t wsl@rocket` drops to an interactive WSL login shell.

The dual-mode behavior comes from a wrapper that branches on
`$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND`: empty (no command supplied → interactive intent) execs
an interactive login shell; non-empty execs `bash -c` with the client command.
The differences from brad@rocket fall in a few places.

```
#!/bin/bash
if [ -z "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND" ]; then
  exec /bin/bash --login -i
else
  exec /bin/bash --login -c "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"
fi
```

child) and hands the *value* to the inner login `bash -c`, which parses it
normally; in the empty branch it execs an interactive login shell. The authkeys
forced-command points cmd.exe at the wrapper via Cygwin bash, passing the script
as a POSIX path:

```

```

ACL-lock the wrapper to the StrictModes allowlist **plus the account's own
read+execute** — `SYSTEM:F`, `BUILTIN\Administrators:F`, `rocket\cygwin:(RX)`
— exactly as the wsl wrapper carries `wsl:RX`. This grant is load-bearing and
easy to miss: the forced command runs as the unprivileged `cygwin` user, so
testing the wrapper *as admin* passes while the real SSH connection fails
`Permission denied` on read. **Validate through the target account, never as
admin.** Verified 2026-05-31: one-shot `ssh cygwin@rocket 'echo hi | tr a-z
A-Z'` → `HI`; interactive (no command) lands a live Cygwin `bash --login -i`.

input.old_string:
**wsl@rocket.** Embedding the wsl.exe invocation directly in the
authkeys command= field works in principle but compounds escape layers
(the inner `$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` must survive sshd's authkeys parsing,
cmd.exe's interpretation, and wsl.exe's argument splitting). A wrapper
`.cmd` file at `C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\wsl\runcmd.cmd` isolates the
runtime concern:

```
@echo off
set WSLENV=SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND/u
C:\Windows\System32\wsl.exe -d Ubuntu-24.04 -u root -- bash -lc "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"
```

The authkeys forced-command then becomes:

```
command="C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\wsl\runcmd.cmd"
```

The wrapper does two load-bearing things:

- `set WSLENV=SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND/u` tells WSL to forward
  `SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` into the Linux side as a Unix-style env var.
  By default WSL only shares a fixed subset of env vars across the
  Windows ↔ Linux boundary; arbitrary vars require an explicit `WSLENV`
  declaration.
- The `"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"` inside the inner `bash -lc` argument is
  literal text at the Windows-side cmd.exe layer (cmd.exe does not
  expand `$`-style variables) and gets expanded later by the WSL bash,
  which sees the value WSLENV forwarded.

The wrapper file is ACL-locked to the same allowlist as authkeys
(`SYSTEM:F`, `BUILTIN\Administrators:F`, plus `wsl:RX`).
input.new_string:
**wsl@rocket.** Embedding the wsl.exe invocation directly in the authkeys
command= field works in principle but compounds escape layers (the inner
`$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` must survive sshd's authkeys parsing, cmd.exe's
interpretation, and wsl.exe's argument splitting). A wrapper `.cmd` file at
`C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\wsl\runcmd.cmd` isolates the runtime concern. To get
**dual-mode** (one-shot + interactive) without re-introducing that escape
graveyard inside the `.cmd`, keep the conditional on the *Linux* side: the
`.cmd` just invokes a clean script inside the distro.

Linux-side `/usr/local/bin/sshrun` (mode 755, inside the `Ubuntu-24.04` distro,
LF endings):

```
#!/bin/bash
if [ -z "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND" ]; then
  exec bash -li
else
  exec bash -lc "$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND"
fi
```

Windows-side `C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\wsl\runcmd.cmd` (CRLF):

```
@echo off
set WSLENV=SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND/u
C:\Windows\System32\wsl.exe -d Ubuntu-24.04 -u root -- /usr/local/bin/sshrun
```

The authkeys forced-command then becomes:

```
command="C:\ProgramData\ssh\users\wsl\runcmd.cmd"
```

The wrapper does two load-bearing things:

- `set WSLENV=SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND/u` tells WSL to forward
  `SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND` into the Linux side as a Unix-style env var. By
  default WSL only shares a fixed subset of env vars across the Windows ↔ Linux
  boundary; arbitrary vars require an explicit `WSLENV` declaration. `sshrun`
  then reads it natively — no `$`-through-cmd.exe escaping at all.
- Routing the conditional through a Linux file (rather than an inline
  `bash -lc "…"` branch in the `.cmd`) keeps cmd.exe's tokenizer away from the
  nested quotes a conditional would otherwise need.

The `.cmd` wrapper is ACL-locked to the same allowlist as authkeys (`SYSTEM:F`,
`BUILTIN\Administrators:F`, plus `wsl:RX`). **Caveat:** `/usr/local/bin/sshrun`
lives inside the distro, so a distro re-import (e.g. a future garrison
re-provision) wipes it — recreate it as part of any wsl re-provisioning.
Verified 2026-05-31: one-shot `ssh wsl@rocket 'docker --version'` → `Docker
version 29.1.3`; interactive (no command) lands a live WSL `bash -li` as root.
